rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at 24 weeks (3 months after the end of treatment)
rows.duration_additional_info: Treatment lasts for 3 months (12 weeks) with 60 sessions (20 minutes per day, Monday to Friday). A follow-up is conducted 3 months after the treatment ends.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 15 patients (Attexis and verum tDCS); Control: 15 patients (Attexis and sham tDCS)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Both arms receive three-month access to the online-based psychotherapy app Attexis. The experimental arm receives simultaneous treatment with a verum tDCS device, while the placebo comparator arm receives a sham tDCS device.
rows.collected_data: Compliance (number of days of treatment), Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale Symptom Checklist (ASRS-v1.1) sumscore and subscales for attention and hyperactivity (including number of responders), and Usability experience questionnaire (UEQ) sumscore.

rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint is assessed at 3 months post-allocation. Follow-up assessment of endpoints occurs at 6 months post-allocation. The attexis program remains accessible for 180 days.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: attexis + TAU; Control: TAU
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm receives access to attexis (a web-based digital health application based on CBT methods) in addition to treatment as usual (TAU). Control arm receives TAU only.
rows.collected_data: ADHD Symptom Severity (ASRS v1.1), Depressive Symptomatology (PHQ-9), Self Esteem (Rosenberg Self Esteem Scale / RSES), Functioning (Work and Social Adjustment Scale / WSAS), Health-Related Quality of Life (AQoL-8D).

rows.dtx_name: attexis - die digitale Therapie bei ADHS im Erwachsenenalter
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome assessed at 3 months post-randomization (T1). Follow-up was conducted at 6 months (T2).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: attexis + Treatment as usual (TAU); Control: TAU alone
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention arm received 'attexis', a fully self-guided digital intervention based on CBT and mindfulness principles, as an adjunct to treatment as usual (TAU). The control arm received TAU alone.
rows.collected_data: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale total score (ADHD symptom severity), functional impairment, depressive symptoms, self-esteem, health-related quality of life, adverse events, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intent-to-treat analyses showed significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at 3 months (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements were also observed across all secondary outcomes, and effects remained stable at 6 months. Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Subgroup analyses demonstrated consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events related to attexis were reported.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were required to use the app for at least 12 weeks and answer questions at four time points over a minimum of 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Access to the full version of the App. Control group: Standard of Care and no application of the App Cara Care für Reizdarm or an alternative App.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Access to the full version of the App. Arm 2 (Control): Standard of Care and no application of the App Cara Care für Reizdarm or an alternative App.
rows.collected_data: Irritable Bowel Syndrome - Severity Scoring System (IBS-SSS), Quality of life (IBS-QOL), Work and activity impairment (WPAI-IBS), Health literacy (HLS-EU-Q16), Perception of anxiety (GAD-7), Perception of depressiveness (PHQ-9). Data was collected using standardized health questionnaires.

rows.dtx_name: companion patella powered by medi - proved by Dt. Kniegesellschaft
rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention consists of a 90-day personalized exercise therapy, with outcomes measured at days 0, 30, 60, and 90.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: DiGA companion patella (app-based therapy). Control: Conventional analog treatment by physiotherapists.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: Patients receive an access code for the DiGA companion patella, which provides a 90-day personalized digital exercise therapy with evidence-based exercises and education. Control arm: Patients receive a doctor's prescription for conventional analog treatment by physiotherapists.
rows.collected_data: Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) for pain reduction and Kujala Score for improvement of knee joint function. Both are measured at 4 time points: day 0, 30, 60, and 90.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is 90 days.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Patients are randomized 1:1 into an intervention group and a control group (total n=270).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: Receives access to the digital health application (DiGA) companion® shoulder, which includes a 90-day personalized exercise therapy with evidence-based exercises and education. Control arm: Receives the standard of care for 90 days, which includes the prescription of physiotherapy (usually 1 to 3 prescriptions with 6 units each).
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Shoulder-specific pain reduction measured with the Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) after 90 days. Secondary Outcomes: Shoulder-specific functional improvement measured with the Shoulder Pain and Disability Index (SPADI) after 90 days; Number of days of incapacity for work due to illness within the survey period measured via self-reporting at day 90.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at three time points: prior to intervention, after completion of the intervention (9 weeks later), and at a 6-month follow-up. Note: The outcomes module lists timeframes of '7 Days' and '14 days', which likely refer to the recall periods of the specific questionnaires.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis online program. Control: Wait-list condition.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis arm: Received immediate access to a web-based intervention consisting of 10 cognitive-behavioral modules (plus introductory and summary modules) organized as simulated dialogues. Wait-list arm: Received access to the Deprexis program after a 6-month delay.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) [Primary Outcome]; WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) [Secondary Outcome]; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (QIDS); Core Self-Evaluations Scale (CSES); Suicidal Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R); Hamburg MS Quality of Life Questionnaire (HALEMS).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The primary outcome is administered every 2 weeks while the participant is engaged in the 8-week online treatment program. The wait list control group waits 8 weeks before accessing the program.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Computerized self-help program for depression (deprexis); Control: Wait List Control.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm receives 8 weeks of access to 'deprexis', a computerized self-help program with 10 CBT-based modules. The Wait List Control arm waits 8 weeks before receiving access to the same program.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report) (QIDS-SR-16), Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D), Inventory of Depression and Anxiety Symptoms (IDAS), Psychiatric Diagnostic Screening Questionnaire (PDSQ), Risky Families Questionnaire (RFQ), Massachusetts General Hospital Antidepressant Treatment History Questionnaire (ATRQ), Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS), and saliva for DNA research.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 10-week treatment period; wait-list group receives access to the intervention after 10 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Three groups: Deprexis (unguided), Deprexis (guided, with therapist), and Wait-list control group.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis (unguided): web-based self-help program without any support from a therapist during the 10-week treatment period. Deprexis (guided, with therapist): web-based self-help program plus scheduled e-mail contact with a therapist during the 10-week treatment period. Wait-list: receives access to Deprexis after 10 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory - II (BDI-II) for depression symptom severity, Brief Symptom Inventory (53-item BSI) for psychopathology severity, Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (IIP), WHOQOL-BREF for quality of life, and CSQ-8 (Client Satisfaction Questionnaire).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Symptoms are assessed over a one-year period, after which the control group also receives the online self-help program.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis (online self-help). Control: Waiting-list control.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm receives 'Deprexis', an online self-help program with and without e-mail support. The control arm receives no intervention initially (waiting-list) but will receive the online self-help after the one-year assessment period.
rows.collected_data: Personal Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HRSD) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms (QIDS) at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants are assessed prior to intervention and eight weeks later. The wait-list group receives access to the intervention after the eight-week period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis (web-based intervention) and Wait-list control group.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis arm: Receives a web-based intervention consisting of 10 online modules (plus one introductory and one summary module) focusing on evidence-based cognitive-behavioral techniques, with each module lasting 10-60 minutes. Wait-list arm: Receives access to Deprexis after an 8-week delay.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) to measure severity of depressive symptoms; WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF) to measure quality of life (physical, psychological, social, environment domains); Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R) used for screening.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The inpatient treatment lasts an average of 6 weeks. The online interventions (deprexis® or placebo information) are used for 90 days.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: deprexis® online self-help program. Control: Online information about depression.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental arm: Patients use the deprexis® online self-help program for 90 days as an additional treatment during their inpatient stay and as an aftercare intervention. Placebo comparator arm: Patients receive online information about depression once a week for 90 days in addition to their inpatient treatment and as an aftercare intervention.
rows.collected_data: Beck's Depression Inventory (BDI-II), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7), Dysfunctional Attitude Scale (DAS), Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE), European Health Interview Survey Quality of Life-8 (EUROHIS-QOL 8), Work Ability Index (WAI) short form, self-devised questionnaires for acceptance and utilization, Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ), OPD-Structure Questionnaire short form (OPD-SFK), Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ), course of mood and depressiveness, utilization of units and other treatments, satisfaction with inpatient treatment, willingness to pay, and remission from depression (BDI score < 13 and Reliable Change Index).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes are assessed at 3, 6, and 12 months from baseline.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Stepped care intervention (SCM); Control: Treatment as usual.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention (SCM): Patients receive differentially intensive treatment according to depression severity. Mild depression: active monitoring, bibliotherapy, online self-help (Deprexis), or telephone-based psychotherapy. Moderate depression: outpatient psychotherapy or psychopharmacological treatment. Severe depression: combined psychotherapy and psychopharmacological treatment, optionally inpatient. Control: Treatment as usual from general physician or other health service providers.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire 9 (PHQ-D-9) for depression severity and response/remission/relapse; Quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) for cost-benefit ratio; EuroQol's EQ-5D and 12-item Short Form Health Survey (SF-12) for quality of life; non-standardized items for health service utilization and medication consumption; Rosenberg self-esteem scale; Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ) for therapeutic alliance; PHQ-D for anxiety, panic symptoms, and somatisation; GAD-7 for general anxiety disorder.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, at week 16 (8 weeks after post-treatment)
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention is an 8-week course of Deprexis. Outcomes are measured at baseline, post-treatment (week 8), and follow-up (week 16).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis (Experimental) and Treatment-as-Usual (Control). The RCT portion (Aim 2a) plans to enroll 132 Veterans.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis arm: Receives an 8-week course of an internet-delivered treatment for depressive symptoms consisting of 12 modules (10 core, 1 intro, 1 summary). Treatment-as-Usual arm: Receives access to standard non-study care.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology (Self-Report; QIDS-SR-16), World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule-II (WHODAS32), Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS), demographic variables, baseline psychopathology, credibility, and Deprexis usage.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: yes, 3 months post-intervention (20 weeks from baseline)
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants are assessed at 4 time points: baseline (T0), 4 weeks mid-treatment (T1), 8 weeks post-intervention (T2), and 3 months post-intervention follow-up (T3).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Experimental group 1: Online program (Deprexis); Experimental group 2: Online program (Deprexis) + online forum; Control group: Wait list.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Experimental group 1 receives the Deprexis web-based self-help program for 8 weeks (at least 30 mins, 1-2 times/week). Experimental group 2 receives the Deprexis program plus access to an online forum (divided into groups of 10 with a moderator) for 8 weeks. The Control group is on a wait list and receives the online program after the 8-week treatment period.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), Fribourg Reward Task, Snaith-Hamilton Pleasure Scale (SHAPS), Behavioral Inhibition System/Behavioral Activation System (BIS/BAS), Perceived Social Support (PSS), Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD), General Self-Efficacy Scale (GSES), 12-Item Short Form Survey (SF-12), Rosenberg Self-Esteem Scale (RSE), Perceived Stress Scale-Short Version (PSS), Somatic Symptom Scale (SSS-8), PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5), Childhood Trauma Questionnaire-Short Form (CTQ-SF), System Usability Scale (SUS), and Client Satisfaction Questionnaire (CSQ).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint is assessed at 3 months. The trial includes an extension phase for maintenance with long-term online follow-up at 6 and 12 months. The waitlist control group waits 6 months before receiving access to the intervention.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Two intervention groups (Deprexis and DeprexisPlus) and one waitlist control group.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Deprexis: Access to the web-based Deprexis program (an online tool based on CBT principles). DeprexisPlus: Access to the web-based Deprexis program plus scheduled e-mail contact (1x/week). Waitlist Control: Wait 6 months for access to the Deprexis program, after which they receive full access.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory-II, WHO Quality of Life scale (WHO-QOL BREF), Multiple Sclerosis Impact Scale (MSIS), Fatigue Scale for Motor and Cognitive Functions (FSMC), Chalder Fatigue Scale, Brief International Cognitive Assessment for Multiple Sclerosis (BICAMS), Suicide Behaviors Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Mini International Neuropsychiatric Interview (MINI).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were randomized to 12 weeks of online self-help or a waiting-list control, with assessments conducted over a 6-month period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis (Online self-help); Control: CAU (Care as usual / waiting-list control)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The experimental arm received 'Deprexis', a 12-week interactive online self-help program. The active comparator arm received Care as Usual (CAU) / waiting-list control.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 items (PHQ-9), Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms - Self-Report (QIDS-SR16), Generalized Anxiety Disorder - 7 (GAD-7), Patient Health Questionnaire - 15 items (PHQ-15), Short Form Health Survey - 12 (SF-12).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention lasts 12 weeks. The primary outcome (costs) is measured over a period of 1 year. Secondary outcomes are measured at baseline (T0), 12 weeks (T1), 6 months (T2), and 12 months (T3).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group uses the program 'Deprexis' for 12 weeks. Control group has no access to 'Deprexis'.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Uses the internet-based self-help program 'Deprexis' for a period of 12 weeks. Arm 2 (Control): Receives no treatment / no access to the 'Deprexis' program.
rows.collected_data: Costs (supplied by the statutory health insurance DAK, excluding ambulant costs); Depression severity (PHQ-9); Health-related quality of life (SF12 and EQ-5D); Social functioning / working and adjustment scale (WSAS); Acceptance and satisfaction with the DAK service and the Deprexis program.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period was 8 weeks. Assessments were conducted prior to (baseline) and after the 8-week intervention period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 140 participants were randomly allocated to either the intervention group or a wait-list control group.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: Received the internet-intervention 'Deprexis' for 8 weeks, consisting of ten therapeutic modules and one summary module based on cognitive-behavioral therapy. Wait-list control arm: Received no additional treatment during the 8-week intervention period (but could continue previously initiated treatments) and gained access to Deprexis after completing the post-survey.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 items depression module (PHQ-9) (Primary Outcome); South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS); Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS); General Anxiety Disorder Screener (GAD-7); Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ); Subjective Appraisal of the intervention; demographic and psychopathological information; clinical anamnesis (e.g., current treatments, use of drugs and self-help, psychiatric diagnoses).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Baseline assessment (T1) and final assessment after 12 weeks (T4) for all arms. The supported intervention group (IG-A) has additional interim assessments after 4 weeks (T2) and 8 weeks (T3).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Supported Intervention Group (IG-A; n ≥ 100), Unsupported Intervention Group (IG-B; n ≥ 100), Waitlist Control Group (CG; n ≥ 100)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: IG-A receives access to the DiGA deprexis® for 12 weeks plus brief structured check-in sessions after 4, 8, and 12 weeks. IG-B receives access to deprexis® for 12 weeks without adjunct therapeutic consultations. CG receives treatment as usual during the 12-week waiting period and gets access to deprexis® afterwards.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale-7 (GAD-7), Symptom Checklist-90-R (SCL-90-R), acceptance of the DiGA (user satisfaction, perceived fit, usefulness), usage intensity, adherence, dropout rates and reasons, subjectively perceived effects and burden (via study-specific questionnaires and structured interviews).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments are conducted at pretreatment, posttreatment (12 weeks), and follow-up (6 months).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Psychotherapy plus web-based online platform (deprexis); Control group: Psychotherapy without Deprexis
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group receives traditional psychotherapy plus access to the deprexis platform, which provides psychoeducational information and exercises based on cognitive-behavioral psychotherapy. Control group receives traditional psychotherapy as usual without Deprexis.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II), 7-item Generalized Anxiety Disorder Scale (GAD-7), somatic symptom module of the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ), Short-Form Health Survey-12 (SF-12), Questionnaire for the evaluation of psychotherapeutic progress (FEP-2), Web Screening Questionnaire (WSQ), Suicidal Behaviours Questionnaire-Revised (SBQ-R), Psychological empowerment scale (adapted from Spreitzer), Depression diagnosis.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants are assessed at three different time points: before receiving access to the treatment, after 10 weeks, and after 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Web-based intervention group and waitlist-control group
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group receives immediate access to a web-based psychological treatment called deprexis, which consists of 10 content modules and is guided by a clinical psychologist. The control group receives access to the same treatment after a waiting period of 10 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Depression levels (BDI-II); clinical depression (Patient Health Questionnaire, Structured Clinical Interview for DSM Diagnosis SCID); Stress perception (Perceived Stress Questionnaire PSQ); Social support (ENRICHD Social Support Inventory ESSI); Quality of life (WHOQOL-BREF); Health behavior; Client satisfaction (Client Satisfaction Questionnaire CSQ).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks and 18 weeks. The control group also completed questionnaires 9 weeks after completing the program. Both groups completed questionnaires 6 months after their respective program usage termination.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Initial treatment group (Deprexis® program) and waitlist control (delayed treatment) group
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: Deprexis® program consisting of ten interactive modules, plus one introductory and one summary module, completed over nine weeks. Control: Waitlist control group that did not receive access to the program initially (delayed treatment).
rows.collected_data: Depressive symptom level measured with the German version of the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI); Work and Social Adjustment Scale (German translation); Subjective usefulness of the program measured by individually designed items.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 8-week course of Deprexis
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis intervention and waitlist control
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received either an 8-week course of the Internet intervention Deprexis or were assigned to a waitlist.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depression Symptoms (QIDS), assessing specific symptoms such as sadness, indecision, early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, agitation, and somatic symptoms.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Deprexis directly improved the symptoms of sadness and indecision. Changes in these symptoms were associated with a change in early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, and agitation. All of these symptoms (except for agitation and early insomnia) showed decreases with Deprexis compared with a waitlist after correcting for multiple comparisons. Six additional symptoms, particularly somatic symptoms, were not impacted by Deprexis compared with a waitlist. The efficacy of Deprexis was due to its direct impact on sadness and indecision.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 12-week period of treatment. No fixed time frame for inpatient treatment duration; post-discharge patients were followed by structured telephone interviews up to the 12-week study endpoint. Measures were carried out at study entry and every 2 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Treatment-as-usual (TAU, N=33) and TAU plus guided deprexis (TAU-PLUS, N=36)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Control arm received treatment-as-usual (TAU). Intervention arm received TAU plus guided deprexis (TAU-PLUS). Both groups received comparable prescribed antidepressant medication.
rows.collected_data: Beck-Depression-Inventory-II (BDI-II), Hamilton-Depression-Scale, Clinical-Global-Impression-Severity, WHO-Well-Being-Index, Helping-Alliance-Questionnaire, working alliance with deprexis, inpatient treatment duration, daily activity, and utilization of post-hospital care after discharge.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, BDI-II scores showed significant between-group differences favoring TAU-PLUS (p=.03, d=-.73, 95% CI -1.4 to .06). TAU-PLUS patients showed greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70, 95% CI -.03 to 1.38) and were discharged significantly earlier (p=.003). Post-discharge, TAU-PLUS reported lower rates of post-hospital care (p=.01) and re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures (including therapist alliance) were not significantly different between groups at endpoint. Working-alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD-improvement and wellbeing.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 12-week online intervention. Secondary outcomes assessed at baseline, post-treatment, and 3-months' and 9-months' follow-up. Primary outcome (costs) evaluated for the study year.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (deprexis) and a control condition
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to either a 12-week online intervention (deprexis) or a control condition.
rows.collected_data: Statutory health insurance costs (estimated using health insurers' administrative data); depression severity, health-related quality of life, and impairment in functioning (assessed via patient self-report).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Total costs of statutory health insurance decreased by 32% from 3139€ per year at baseline to 2119€ in the study year in the intervention group, compared to a 13% mean reduction in the control group (changes differed significantly). The intervention group also showed a significantly greater reduction in depression severity and impairment in functioning, and a significantly greater increase in health-related quality of life compared to the control group.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention group received immediate access to the program for 90 days (approximately 3 months), while the control group received delayed access after 8 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: n=94; Control: n=95
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention condition: treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days. Control condition: treatment as usual and delayed access to Deprexis, after 8 weeks.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), semistructured interview, Clinical Outcome in Routine Evaluation-Outcome Measurement (CORE-OM), perceived self-efficacy measure, login frequency, dropout rates, deterioration rates, demographic and clinical variables, and satisfaction with Deprexis.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the immediate access group logged in an average of 14.81 (SD 12.16) times. The intervention group improved significantly more than the delayed access control group on the primary depression measure (PHQ-9; Cohen d=0.80; P<.001), general psychological state (CORE-OM; Cohen d=0.82; P<.001), and perceived self-efficacy (Cohen d=0.63; P<.001). Remission was achieved by 21% (20/94) of intervention participants compared with 7% (7/95) in the control group (P<.001). Deterioration rates were lower in the immediate access group. The dropout rate was high, but no differences in demographic and clinical variables were found. Participants reported a medium to high level of satisfaction.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 8-week course of treatment immediately or after an 8-week delay (waitlist control).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (immediate treatment) n = 285; Control (waitlist) n = 91
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an 8-week course of Deprexis (an Internet treatment for depression with relatively minimal support) immediately. The control group was a waitlist control that received the treatment after an 8-week delay.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms-Self-Report (QIDS-SR), self-reported symptoms of depression, interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, depression-related disability, symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses indicated that treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported symptoms of depression (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Working alliance was assessed after 6 and 12 weeks. There was a high dropout rate at the 6-month follow-up.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: psychotherapy plus a web-based depression program ('deprexis'); Control: regular psychotherapy
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to one of two active treatment conditions: regular psychotherapy alone or regular psychotherapy combined with a web-based depression program ('deprexis').
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms (primary outcome); anxiety symptoms, somatic symptoms, and quality of life (secondary outcomes); working alliance.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The combination of psychotherapy with the web-based program ('deprexis') was more effective than psychotherapy alone at 12 weeks. There were medium between-group effect sizes on primary depressive symptoms (Cohen's d = .51) and small to medium between-group effect sizes on secondary outcomes (Cohen's d = .07-.55). No negative side effects were observed in the blended format, such as a lower working alliance compared to psychotherapy alone.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Measurements were taken at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and at a 6-month follow-up.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: N = 320; Control: N = 76
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received 9 weeks of immediate-program-access to the Deprexis program as an add-on to treatment-as-usual. The control group received a 9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual condition.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) to measure depression (primary outcome measure); Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSA) to measure social functioning (secondary outcome measure).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Of 396 participants, 216 (55%) completed the post-measurement 9 weeks later. Available case analyses revealed a significant reduction in depression severity (BDI), Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.94), and significant improvement in social functioning (WSA), Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.95). These improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses confirmed significant effects on depression and social functioning improvements (BDI: Cohen's d = .30, CI 95% = 0.05 - 0.55; WSA: Cohen's d = .36, CI 95% = 0.10 - 0.61). A much higher percentage of patients in the intervention group experienced a significant reduction of depression symptoms (BDI: odds ratio [OR] = 6.8, CI 95% = 2.90 - 18.19) and recovered more often (OR = 17.3, 95% CI 2.3 - 130). More than 80% of the users felt subjectively that the program had been helpful.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome is assessed at 3 months post randomization. All outcomes are assessed again at a secondary endpoint 6 months post randomization.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: regular psychotherapy plus a web-based depression program ('deprexis'); Control: exclusively receiving regular psychotherapy.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants are randomized within therapists to one of two conditions: regular psychotherapy plus the 'deprexis' web-based program, or a control condition receiving only regular psychotherapy.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms, indicators of anxiety, somatic symptoms, quality of life, working alliance, and feasibility/acceptability of the treatment condition.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were given access to Deprexis for 12 weeks. Randomization occurred after the first 2 weeks. TILS was administered once a week for 4 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis + TILS; Control: Deprexis + sham/placebo (40 participants total randomized between the two groups)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants received Deprexis (internet-based CBT). The intervention group received TILS to the right forehead once a week for 4 weeks, while the control group received a sham/placebo.
rows.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) scores; adverse effects.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: There was a 43% reduction in QIDS-SR scores in the sham group from baseline to week 12, compared to a 56% reduction in the TILS group (an additional 30% reduction, [56-43]/43 = 30%). Participants receiving TILS showed a significantly greater reduction of depressive symptoms than those receiving sham/placebo. No adverse effects were reported.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Re-assessment occurred after 8 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group and wait-list control group
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an online-intervention for depression ('Deprexis'). The control group was a wait-list control group.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 (PHQ-9) (primary outcome), Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS), and South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses showed that the intervention led to a significant reduction in depressive symptoms as well as gambling-related symptoms compared to the control group, with moderate to strong effect sizes. PP analyses failed to yield significant results due to high rates of non-completion and limited statistical power. Moderator analyses indicated that Deprexis was particularly beneficial in reducing problematic gambling for those scoring high on baseline gambling-related symptoms and for those who gamble due to loneliness.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Intermediary and long-term assessments were conducted over 36 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Internet intervention for depression (Deprexis) compared with care as usual.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One arm received an Internet intervention for depression (Deprexis) and the control arm received care as usual.
rows.collected_data: Depressive symptoms (including worthlessness, fatigue, depressed mood, and concentration) and quality of life aspects (including overall impairment through emotional problems and activity level).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Item-level analysis showed that scale-level effects can be explained by small item-level effects on most depressive symptoms at all points of assessment. Higher scores on these items at baseline predicted overall symptom reduction throughout the whole assessment period. Deprexis directly affected certain symptoms of depression (e.g., worthlessness and fatigue) and certain aspects of the quality of life (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), while other domains were affected indirectly (e.g., depressed mood and concentration as well as activity level).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed on patients' self-report at baseline, three months, and six months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (deprexis + care as usual) and Control group (care as usual)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week web-based cognitive behavioral intervention ('deprexis') in addition to care as usual. Control group received care as usual.
rows.collected_data: Health-related resource use, use of medication, incapacity to work, and direct health care costs (assessed via patients' self-report).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Total direct health care costs decreased in both groups during the study period, but changes from baseline did not significantly differ between study groups. Costs for psychotherapeutic treatment decreased in the intervention group (-16.8% (€80)) whereas they increased in the control group (+14.7% (€60)) (tdf=685=2.57; p=0.008).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Patients were allocated to their respective groups for a 9-week period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n=45 allocated, 35 completed) and waitlist control group (n=45 allocated, 36 completed)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis, a fully automated, internet-based, cognitive behavioural therapy programme. The control group was a waitlist control.
rows.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI); adverse events (suicidal ideation).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 71 patients completed the trial. During the intervention, BDI scores decreased in the Deprexis group and increased in the control group, yielding a positive effect of Deprexis relative to the waitlist group (mean group difference -4.02 points [95% CI -7.26 to -0.79], p=0.015, effect size d=0.53). Worsening of depressive symptoms from below to above the clinical cutoff (BDI >13) occurred in 3 (7%) of 45 patients in the control group and 0 patients in the Deprexis group. There were no adverse events with respect to new occurrence of suicidal ideation.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Symptoms were assessed pre and post intervention at 12 weeks.
rows.study_size_participants: 509 in the intervention group (total study size not specified in the abstract)
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Care as usual (CAU) plus the Internet intervention Deprexis (n=509); Control: Care as usual (CAU)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received CAU plus the Internet intervention Deprexis. Participants with moderate symptom severity at baseline also received e-mail support (guidance). Control group received care as usual (CAU).
rows.collected_data: Depression symptoms, patient characteristics, self-rating screening for mental disorders, attitudes towards online interventions, quality of life, and adherence (usage time and number of sessions).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Adherence was good with an average of 7 hours of usage time and 8 sessions. Patient characteristics (age, sex, depressive symptoms, and confidence in the program's effectiveness) predicted a higher number of sessions (explaining 15-25% of variance). Older age (β = .16) and higher depressive symptoms (β = .15) were associated with higher usage duration. Higher adherence predicted greater reduction in depressive symptoms over 12 weeks (number of sessions: β = .13, usage duration: β = .14), though this was mostly explained by receiving guidance (β = .27 and .26). Overall, older age was associated with adherence, and adherence was positively associated with outcome, although the effects were small.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 12 weeks post-test. Secondary outcomes assessed at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: n=509; Control group: n=504
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an online intervention (Deprexis) in addition to care as usual (CAU). The control group received CAU alone.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression, Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms, and sociodemographic variables (sex, age, educational attainment, employment status, relationship status, and lifetime frequency of episodes).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Between-group differences were observed in post-test scores, indicating the effectiveness of Deprexis. The effects of the intervention were demonstrated across all subgroups. After exploring moderating effects based on linear regression models, none of the selected sociodemographic variables was found to be moderating treatment outcomes. The findings suggest that Deprexis is equally beneficial to a wide range of people, and participant characteristics were not associated with treatment benefits.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis + care-as-usual): n = 509; Control (care-as-usual): n = 504
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group accessed the Internet intervention Deprexis adjunctively to care-as-usual. The control group had access to care-as-usual.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) scores; Web Screening Questionnaire (assessing anxiety-related comorbidities including generalized anxiety disorder, social phobia, panic disorder, agoraphobia, panic disorder with agoraphobia, specific phobia, posttraumatic stress disorder, and obsessive-compulsive disorder).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Out of 12 potential moderators examined, only social phobia moderated the intervention effect on depression outcome in favor of Deprexis (p < .05 for both continuous PHQ-9 scores and clinically important PHQ-9 changes). However, the moderating effects did not reach small effect sizes, accounted for less than 1% of the variance in change in depressive symptoms, and showed limited reproducibility in randomly selected split halves.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline and posttreatment.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The study used data from a trial examining a guided Internet intervention (deprexis) for patients with mild to moderate depression.
rows.collected_data: Working Alliance Inventory adapted for guided Internet interventions (WAI-I), Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (ZUF-8), and Attitudes toward Psychological Online-Interventions Questionnaire (APOI).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The results suggested a two-factor solution for the WAI-I, with a very good model fit, evidence of factor independency, adequate internal consistency, and external validity for the complete scale and the sub-scales. The WAI-I was shown to be a reliable and valid instrument to capture alliance in guided Internet interventions.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Early change was assessed in the first four weeks of the intervention. Long-term outcome (onset of remission) was tracked over a 36-month (3-year) follow-up period.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants used the web-based intervention 'deprexis' for mild to moderate depressive symptoms.
rows.collected_data: Baseline parameters/variables, early change in depressive symptoms (assessed in the first four weeks), and onset of remission over 36 months.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Early change class was a significant predictor of remission over 36 months. Compared to early deterioration after screening, both early response after registration and after screening were associated with a higher likelihood of remission. In sensitivity and secondary analyses, only change class consistently emerged as a predictor of long-term outcome. Those showing early improvement had a higher likelihood of long-term remission.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants completed the PHQ-9 biweekly during the 12-week treatment.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Participants were randomized to a 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis); control group details are not specified in the abstract.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis) for patients with mild-to-moderate depression symptoms.
rows.collected_data: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) assessed biweekly during treatment; dropout rates/probability; reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Four change-dropout patterns were identified: C3 (N=134) had high impairment, improvement, and low dropout probability, with the highest rate of reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up. C2 (N=32) had high impairment, deterioration, and high dropout probability. C1 (N=198) had low impairment, improvement, and high dropout probability. C4 (N=119) had high impairment, no change, and low dropout probability. The study concluded that both deterioration and rapid improvement (perceived 'good enough' dosage) may lead to dropout.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment) and 6 months (follow-up).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 12-week Psychological online intervention (Deprexis); Control: Care as usual (CAU)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to either a 12-week psychological online intervention (Deprexis) or care as usual (CAU).
rows.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9), intervention use (frequency and duration), and attitudes towards psychological online interventions (POIs).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Compared to Millennials, Boomers used the intervention significantly more often (d = 0.45) and for a longer duration (d = 0.46), and endorsed more positive attitudes towards POIs (d = 0.14). There was no significant Age Group by Intervention Group interaction for change in PHQ-9. The post-assessment between-group effect size (intervention vs. CAU control) for Millennials and Boomers were d = 0.26 and d = 0.39, respectively, and were stable at follow-up (d = 0.37 and d = 0.39). The POI was superior to CAU and was comparably effective among Millennials and Baby Boomers.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis) and Control group (care as usual)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the internet-based intervention Deprexis, while the control group received care as usual. All participants in both conditions were permitted to use any type of treatment.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) to assess nine depressive symptoms and overall depression severity.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Seven of the nine considered symptoms showed larger improvements in the intervention condition relative to care as usual (effect sizes ranging from 0.15 to 0.31). No significant differences were found for the two other symptoms. Network estimation suggested that four of the seven symptom-specific effects were direct, whereas three were indirect. Exploratory analyses showed the intervention was more effective in improving overall depression severity for participants with higher scores on the four symptoms directly affected by the intervention.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 3 months intervention period
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (deprexis program) and a control group (care as usual)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the depression-focused Internet intervention program 'deprexis', while the control group received care as usual.
rows.collected_data: Attitudes toward Internet interventions (assessed pre to post over a 3 months intervention period), efficacy of the program, and usage time.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Positive initial attitudes toward Internet interventions were associated with greater efficacy (η2p = .014) independent of usage time, whereas a negative attitude (perceived lack of personal contact) was associated with reduced efficacy (η2p = .012). Users' attitudes changed during the trial, and both the magnitude and direction of attitude change were associated with the efficacy of the program over time (η2p = .030).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment) and 6 months (follow-up).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) adjunctive to usual care; Control: Care as usual alone (CAU)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to either care as usual alone (CAU) or a 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) adjunctive to usual care.
rows.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for self-rated depression severity.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001). The post-assessment between-group effect size in favour of the intervention was d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13-0.64) and was stable at follow-up with d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06-0.69). The rate of participants experiencing at least minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%) with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5-10).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, post-treatment (after the 12-week intervention), and at a 6-month follow-up.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Augmented therapy group (n = 166) and standard psychotherapy group (n = 174)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The augmented therapy group received a 12-week minimally guided Internet intervention (Deprexis) in addition to routine outpatient psychotherapy. The standard psychotherapy group received routine outpatient psychotherapy only.
rows.collected_data: Symptoms of depression, therapeutic progress, mental health-related quality of life, physical health-related quality of life, and rates of clinical improvement.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses indicated that combined treatment led to a greater reduction in symptoms of depression (effect size d = 0.32; p = .002), improved therapeutic progress (d = 0.36; p = .003), and higher mental health-related quality of life (d = 0.34; p = .004). There was no intervention effect on physical health-related quality of life. The same pattern was found at 6-month follow-up, and adjunctive treatment also resulted in increased rates of clinical improvement.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 12-week internet intervention with regular assessments over a twelve-month period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (internet intervention plus usual care) and Control group (care as usual alone). Total N = 1013.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care. Control arm received care as usual alone.
rows.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9), remission rates, and antidepressant medication usage.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Remission rates over time were significantly higher in the intervention group (HR 1.31; p = 0.009). The intervention was more effective in the subgroup not taking antidepressant medication (HR 1.88; p < 0.001). PHQ-change from baseline was greater in the intervention group (p < 0.001) with the between-group effect gradually decreasing from d = 0.36 at three months to d = 0.13 at twelve months (group by time interaction: p < 0.001).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The internet intervention lasted 12 weeks. The primary outcome measure was assessed at 3 months and 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Care-as-usual alone (control) and a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care (intervention).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Control arm received care-as-usual alone. Intervention arm received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care.
rows.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (Patient Health Questionnaire-9); demographic and clinical parameters; attitudes towards internet interventions (Attitudes towards Psychological Online Interventions Questionnaire).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The recruitment source was only associated with very few of the examined demographic and clinical characteristics. Compared with participants recruited from clinical sources, participants recruited through insurance companies were more likely to be employed. Clinically recruited participants were as severely affected as those from other recruitment sources but more sceptical of internet interventions. The effectiveness of the intervention was not differentially associated with recruitment source (treatment by recruitment source interaction=0.28, p=0.84). The results support the hypothesis that the intervention is effective across different recruitment sources including clinical settings.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

rows.key_outcomes_findings: Intent-to-treat (ITT) analyses revealed significant improvements in fatigue in the intervention group compared to the control group after 3 months (Cohen’s d: 0.29) and 6 months (Cohen’s d: 0.31). Participants in the intervention group also reported significantly lower fear of cancer progression than the control group at 3 and 6 months, and lower levels of distress symptoms at 6 months. No between-group differences were found for health-related QoL.

rows.collected_data: Clinician-rated short version of the Montgomery Asberg-Depression Scale (svMADRS), Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), and intervention usage (number of sessions).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: 83.6% of patients (n=87) used the intervention at least once, with a mean of 18.05 sessions (SD=11.33) among users. Only 8.0% (n=7) received the guided version. Depressive symptoms decreased significantly over the observation period: svMADRS scores decreased from 29.72 (SD=10.03) to 15.73 (SD=9.74) (Cohen's d=1.42, 95% CI 0.08-2.76) and PHQ-9 scores decreased from 15.20 (SD=5.03) to 8.77 (SD=5.03) (d=1.29, 95% CI 0.60-1.97).

rows.key_outcomes_findings: Compared with the control group, significantly greater reductions in Chalder Fatigue Scale scores were seen in the ELEVIDA group at week 12 (primary endpoint, intention-to-treat analysis: between-group mean difference 2.74 points; 95% CI 1.16 to 4.32; p=0.0007; effect size d=0.53). These effects were sustained at week 24 (intention-to-treat analysis: between-group mean difference 2.19 points; 95% CI 0.57 to 3.82; p=0.0080).

rows.key_outcomes_findings: The interventional group showed positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscale compared to the standard of care group. Statistically significant pain reduction was seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068) and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). Significant reduction of symptoms at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036) and t6 (p = 0.0377) were recognized. These findings are further supported by the significant benefit of the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5 and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009) and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery.

All participants receive structured physiotherapy and use the 'Orthopy' app starting in the third postoperative week.

rows.collected_data: Knee injury and osteoarthritis outcome score (KOOS) including subscales for Pain, Symptoms, Quality of life, Activities of daily living, and Sport; subjective pain and function levels via numeric rating scale (NRS).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The interventional group showed statistically significant pain reduction at t1 (p=0.0005), t2 (p=0.0068), and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p=0.01). A significant reduction of symptoms was seen at t1 (p=0.0001), t2 (p=0.0036), and t6 (p=0.0377). There was a significant benefit on the pain NRS at t4. KOOS subscales for quality of life (p=0.0066), activities of daily living (p=0.0009), and Sport (p=0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery. Combining the Orthopy app and standard of care significantly improved prehabilitation and rehabilitation compared with isolated standard of care.

rows.key_outcomes_findings: Pain reduction was significantly larger in re.flex than in usual care, with an adjusted mean difference between study groups of 4.8 (95% CI 0.7-8.9; P=.02; Cohen d=0.35) points. Improvement in physical function was not statistically significant (beta coefficient [β]=3.9 points, 95% CI 0.0-7.9, P=.049). A total of 12 adverse events were linked to re.flex, none of which were serious. Participants adhered to 77% (2705/3528) of all scheduled exercise sessions.

The control group (CG) received no study intervention but used usual care.
rows.collected_data: Osteoarthritis-specific pain (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score [KOOS]), all other KOOS subscales, general health-related quality of life (Veterans RAND 12-item Health Survey), psychological measures (e.g., exercise self-efficacy), performance measures (strength and postural control), adherence, and safety.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Statistically significant effects in favor of the IG for pain reduction (P<.001; ES=0.76), improvements in physical function (P<.001; ES=0.64), symptoms (P=.01; ES=0.53), sport and recreation activities (P=.02; ES=0.47), knee-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.76), and the physical component of general health-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.74). Mean differences ranged from 6.0 to 13.2 points. No effects were found for psychological and performance measures. Participants adhered to 92.5% (899/972) of all scheduled exercise sessions.

Both groups wore an activity tracker on their wrist for the 8-week duration.
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Insomnia severity measured by the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI) at 8 & 16 weeks post-randomization. Secondary Outcomes: Sleep continuity (measured with a sleep diary and actigraphy continuously for 8 weeks); Dysfunctional beliefs about sleep (DBAS; 8 & 16 weeks); Pre-Sleep Arousal (PSAS; 8 & 16 weeks); Daytime well-being (WHO-5; 8 & 16 weeks); Quality of life (WHQOL_BREF; 8 & 16 weeks); Symptoms of anxiety and depression (HADS; 8 & 16 weeks).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital cognitive behavioural therapy arm, relative to digital sleep monitoring. Treatment effects in favour of digital cognitive behavioural therapy were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables, indicating that sleep improved throughout the 8-week intervention period.

The therapy consists of 11 modules (approx. 20 mins each, 3.5 hours total). The control group was placed on a waitlist plus care-as-usual (CAU).
rows.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), DBAS-16 (dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep), FSS (Fatigue Severity Index), ESS (Epworth Sleepiness Scale), CES-D (Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale, short version), STAI-T (State-Trait Anxiety Inventory), WHOQOL_BREF (WHO Quality of Life Questionnaire, brief version), WHO-5 (WHO-Five Well-Being Index), MADRE (Mannheim Dream Questionnaire), DTI (Düsseldorf Dream Inventory), NDQ (Nightmare Distress Questionnaire), CAN (Cognitive Appraisal of Nightmares).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At posttreatment (8 weeks), dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in the ISI (adjusted difference = -7.60) compared to the waitlist control (d = -2.08). Clinical improvement was reflected in responder and remission rates. Treatment effects were also observed for daytime functioning, quality of life, symptoms of depression and anxiety (ds = 0.26-1.02), and at long-term follow-up for the intervention group (ds = 0.18-1.65). No effects were found for dream and nightmare frequency.

Control group was a waiting list control (WLC).
rows.collected_data: Severity of depressive symptoms (Patient Health Questionnaire-9; PHQ-9), insomnia severity, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, well-being, and mechanistic effect measures.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Large treatment effects at 12- and 24 weeks showed significant reductions in depressive symptoms (-3.34 and -2.83; ps <0.001; ds = 0.66-0.78) in the dCBT-I group. Treatment effects in favor of dCBT-I were also found for insomnia severity (ds = 1.46-1.94) and most secondary outcomes (ds = 0.33-1.14). The study demonstrates that digital dCBT-I can be effective for individuals with depression and comorbid insomnia, with stable effects.
