rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments took place at baseline, 8 and 24 weeks post-randomization.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: dCBT-I (n = 103) and WLC (n = 104)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized (1:1) to either digital cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (dCBT-I) added to regular treatment of chronic pain, or a waitlist control (WLC) condition.
rows.collected_data: Insomnia severity (Insomnia Severity Index [ISI]), sleep- and pain-related symptoms, measures of well-being and dreams, daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The dCBT-I group showed large improvements in the severity of insomnia compared to the WLC both after 8 weeks (-4.36, p < 0.001; d = -1.18) and 24 weeks (-4.88, p < 0.001; d = -1.32). Pain-related impairments and life control also improved, favoring dCBT-I (ds = 0.22-0.35). Moderate-to-large treatment effects were also observed for secondary outcomes, including daytime sleepiness, fatigue, and dysfunctional beliefs and attitudes about sleep (ds = 0.47-1.12).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class IIa according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: 8 weeks of intervention; assessments at baseline, 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: digital cognitive behavioural therapy (n = 29) and digital sleep monitoring (n = 27)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomly allocated (1:1) to 8 weeks of digital cognitive behavioural therapy or 8 weeks of digital sleep monitoring (control application).
rows.collected_data: Insomnia severity; self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables via application-integrated sleep diaries and actigraphy.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87-1.08) indicated robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital cognitive behavioural therapy arm relative to digital sleep monitoring. Treatment effects in favour of digital cognitive behavioural therapy were also found for self-reported and actigraphy-derived sleep continuity variables.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants in the intervention group are expected to exercise with the app for 12 weeks, optimally for 20 minutes 3 times a week.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Group 1 (Intervention): ViViRA digital health app. Group 2 (Control): Physiotherapy max. once a week.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group receives a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app to exercise for 12 weeks (recommended 20 minutes, 3 times a week) and a wristband sensor to measure physical activity. Control group undergoes physiotherapy max. once a week as standard of care.
rows.collected_data: Clinical disease markers: BASMI (mobility), BASFI (function), BASDAI, ASDAS, and ASAS 20 (disease activity); physical activity evaluated by activity sensor; movement and gait analysis with sensors; BSA, WPAI, PSQI, ASqL, SF-36, HAQ, PAIN, TSK, IPAQ, and PAHCO; clinical routine including serology blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation), biochemical markers, physical examination, and bone resorption markers (CTX, osteocalcin, BAP); hand strength.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were measured after 2, 6, and 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app. Control: Conventional physiotherapy.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app, including execution of exercises and educations according to guidelines, digitally adapted to the individual performance of the patient. Arm 2 (Control): Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists analogue to the agreements with the German statutory health insurance system.
rows.collected_data: Pain reduction measured by VNRS (Verbal Numeric Rating Scale); Function improvement of mobility.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcomes were assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received ViViRA DHA; Control group received standard physiotherapy.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group: ViViRA Digital Health Application (DHA) for home-based physiotherapy. Control group: Standard physiotherapy.
rows.collected_data: Pain (verbal rating scale [VRS], PAIN-Detect), quality of life (SF-36), and mobility (BASMI).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline: 1.1 [range 0.7-1.5]; follow-up: 1.0 [range 0.5-1.4]; p = 0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline: 1.5 [range 1.1-1.9]; follow-up: 1.8 [range 1.4-2.2]; p = 0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level at week 3.5 ± 2.8) than did the control group (VRS pain level at week 4.5 ± 2) after 12 weeks. DHA use resulted in superior outcomes compared to standard physiotherapy.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise for 6 months. Assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention groups: ViViRA (with personal coaching) and Kaia Health (with AI-based coaching). Control group: ViViRA (without coaching).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Personal coaching (ViViRA) consisted of a one-time, 30-min online coaching session prior to using the digital health application (DHA). AI coaching (Kaia Health) consisted of video-based AI integrated into the DHA to provide movement guidance during each session. The control group used the ViViRA DHA without any coaching.
rows.collected_data: Sociodemographic data, questionnaires, mobility (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index [BASMI]), pain intensity (PainDETECT for neuropathic pain), Physical Activity-related Health Competence (PAHCO), and adherence (frequency of DHA use).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed a significant improvement in mobility (BL-3 month: mean BASMI change -0.6 to -0.7; all p < 0.001). Pain intensity decreased substantially in all arms (BL-6 month: mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points; all p <= 0.006). PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (BL-6 month: +1.02, p = 0.013) but did not exceed the other interventions in a direct comparison. Overall, none of the coaching strategies showed significant superiority over the stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: The digital home exercise program lasted 12 weeks; physiotherapy included 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 108 participants; Control group: 105 participants
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: 12-week stand-alone digital home exercise program including 4 exercises daily. Control: standard of care physiotherapy including 6 to 12 sessions, depending on the severity of symptoms.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity assessed using a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS); clinical relevance of pain reduction (thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the VNRS and a pain reduction of at least 30%); retention rates.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At 12 weeks, the mean difference in pain scores between the 2 groups was -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. The intervention group achieved a clinically relevant reduction in pain (mean change -3.35 [SD 2.05] points or -53.1% [SD 29.5]). The control group did not reach clinical relevance (mean change -0.91 [SD 1.5] points or -14.6% [SD 25.3]). Retention rates were 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group. The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity compared with standard care.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with intra-individual control (the control is the preliminary/historical findings of the same patient).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app (In-Home Therapeutic Training Program).
rows.collected_data: Pain using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS) after 12 weeks; Proprietary functional test / series of functional scores.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Patients who discontinued without a pain score after week 2 were considered study users but not ITT users.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with intraindividual control (the control is the pre-finding/historical finding of the same patient).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app. The study is single-armed, with the control being the historical baseline finding of the same patient.
rows.collected_data: Pain measured using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS); proprietary functional test / series of functional scores; information about well-being and configuration needs.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm observational study. All participants received the digital therapeutic (Vivira).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intra-individual pre-post comparison of patients with non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program.
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Self-reported pain intensity, assessed with a verbal-numerical rating scale (VNRS). Secondary Outcome: Physical level of activity, assessed in the dimensions of mobility, strength and coordination based on a discrete scale. Patient characteristics for treatment adherence.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Observation period of 12 weeks, with assessments at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 3629 patients. No separate control group is described (retrospective observational study with time-series and matched-pair analysis).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants used the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira' for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain score (assessed with a verbal numerical rating scale) and self-reported function scores.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (Skillings-Mack test T3628=5308; P<.001), which were within the range of a clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive yet more variable response across the pain areas (back, hip, and knee).

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.duration_additional_info: Pain score changes were assessed over the intended 12-week duration of the program.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 2517 participants using the Vivira smartphone-based program (analyzed using an incomplete matched block design).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants used Vivira, a smartphone-based digital home exercise program for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores (0-10 scale), self-reported functional scores (strength, mobility, coordination), and adherence rates.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19 out of 10 (SD 1.96) to an average of 3.35 out of 10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores, whereas participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in their coordination scores. A high yet expected attrition rate was observed.

rows.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
rows.intervention_and_control_group: High use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), and sub-LU use profiles of a digital home exercise program.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Patients were categorized into four use profiles based on their adherence to a digital home exercise program: high use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), or sub-LU.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensities; achievement of clinically relevant pain improvement.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles show a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement of self-reported pain intensities. Additionally, the achievement of any higher use level is associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement.

rows.duration_additional_info: The Caterna visual training games are played for at least 90 days (30-45 minutes daily). The prospective data analysis will take place over a period of 5 years.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: One observational group receiving Caterna online visual training in addition to standard occlusion therapy.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Amblyopia therapy with the use of Caterna online visual training, used in addition to occlusion therapy. The games are played on the computer for 90 days daily for 30-45 minutes during occlusion.
rows.collected_data: Visual acuity (measured during regular check-ups by orthoptists and ophthalmologists to determine if the worse eye reaches the same visual acuity as the better eye or if there is no further improvement).

rows.duration_additional_info: Patients had stagnation of visual acuity despite at least 90 days of occlusion therapy prior to the study.
rows.study_size_participants: 227 children initially; 105 patients (114 eyes) analyzed after propensity score matching
rows.intervention_and_control_group: FAVAS group = 57 eyes; Control group = 57 eyes
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: FAVAS group: received focal ambient visual acuity stimulation (FAVAS) integrated into a computer game alongside occlusion therapy. Control group: received occlusion therapy alone.
rows.collected_data: Best corrected visual acuity (BCVA, measured in logMAR); stereo vision.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The FAVAS group showed a significant improvement in BCVA, with a mean gain of 0.15 logMAR (95% CI [0.11;0.18], p < 0.001), compared to a non-significant improvement of 0.01 logMAR (95% CI [-0.02;0.04], p = 0.356) in the control group. No significant changes were observed in stereo vision in either group. The combination of occlusion therapy and FAVAS was superior to occlusion alone.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: The VR system is worn for approximately 5-10 minutes during the dental sealant application. The study is a single-visit split-mouth crossover design.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: All participants received both Virtual Reality (VR) and Basic Behavior Guidance (BBG) in a split-mouth crossover design. Total N = 20.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Group 1: VR with right side sealants first, then BBG with left side. Group 2: VR with left side sealants first, then BBG with right side. Group 3: BBG with right side sealants first, then VR with left side. Group 4: BBG with left side sealants first, then VR with right side.
rows.collected_data: Heart rate (beats per minute) at baseline and during sealant placement; Frankl behavior score (1-5); Healthcare Provider Questionnaire; demographic factors; hours of screen time per week; parent and patient satisfaction survey post-treatment.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Mean change in heart rate (beats per minute) with VR: Group 1 (VR right first) = -2; Group 2 (VR left first) = -6.25; Group 3 (BBG right first) = -8.8; Group 4 (BBG left first) = -6.25. Mean change in heart rate with BBG: Group 1 = -2; Group 2 = -2.75; Group 3 = -3; Group 4 = -0.5. No statistical analysis was conducted except mean and range.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.follow_up_after_primary_end_point: Yes, adverse events were tracked for 4 months for each patient.
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary and secondary outcomes were assessed over a 90-day (approx. 13 weeks) postoperative period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Standard of Care + RelieVRx Group) = 31; Control (Standard of Care Group) = 32
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The Control group received standard of care multi-modality pain management (combination of medications such as local anesthesia, ibuprofen, acetaminophen, gabapentin, ketamine, lidocaine, and opioids). The Intervention group received the same standard of care pain management plus the RelieVRx virtual reality headset.
rows.collected_data: Visual Analog Pain scale (0-10) for acute postoperative pain; Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME) for opioid consumption; KOOS Jr. Score (0-100) for knee injury and osteoarthritis outcomes; Veterans RAND 12 Health Survey (VR-12) Physical Component Score for quality of life; Numerical Pain Rating Scale (0-10) at baseline; Adverse events.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Acute Postoperative Pain (Visual Analog Pain scale, 0-10): Intervention mean 4.42 (SD 1.17) vs Control mean 5.13 (SD 1.79). Opioid Consumption (median change in total MME from pre-op to post-op): Intervention -34.8 (IQR -37.0 to -23.5) vs Control -28.0 (IQR -35.0 to -23.5). KOOS Jr. Score (median change): Intervention 32.2 (IQR 21.2 to 42.0) vs Control 27.6 (IQR 17.5 to 38.0). VR-12 Physical Component Score (median change): Intervention 9.5 (IQR 4.0 to 12.8) vs Control 7.2 (IQR 2.0 to 11.4). No deaths, serious adverse events, or other adverse events were reported in either group. (Note: No p-values or statistical significance levels were reported).

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: Evaluated at day 56 (end of treatment).
rows.intervention_and_control_group: RelieVRx (n=536) and Sham (n=531)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: RelieVRx: 8-week, self-administered in-home, behavioral skills virtual reality program that trains diaphragmatic breathing, biofeedback, cognition and emotion regulation, mindfulness, and pain education skills. Control: strong active Sham.
rows.collected_data: Brief pain inventory, pain intensity, and pain interference.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: RelieVRx was superior to Sham for pain intensity and pain interference reductions from pretreatment to day 56 (difference from Sham, pain intensity: 0.406 [0.170-0.642] and pain interference: 0.523 [0.285-0.760]). Pain intensity and interference reductions for RelieVRx at day 56 were clinically meaningful (pain intensity: 2.0 [out of 10] points [1.73-2.06], pain interference: 2.3 points [1.99-2.33]).

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention consisted of a 56-session program. Analyses were conducted from pretreatment to 12 months posttreatment.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Skills-Based VR (RelieVRx); Control: active Sham
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received a 56-session, self-administered in-home, Skills-Based VR program for chronic low back pain (RelieVRx). Control arm received an active Sham.
rows.collected_data: Average pain intensity and pain interference scores (measured on a 0-10 scale).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: From baseline to 12 months posttreatment, Skills-Based VR reductions for average pain intensity (1.7 ± 2.1) and pain interference (1.9 ± 2.3) were robust and significantly greater than those found for Sham. More than half of Skills-Based VR participants reported at least a 2-point reduction in pain intensity, pain interference, or both at 12 months posttreatment.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: The intervention period is 9 weeks, with primary outcomes assessed at 9 weeks and 6 months post-intervention.

rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: Received a self-guided digital program of CBT-I (Somryst) culturally adapted for Spanish-language speakers, consisting of six cores over 9 weeks, plus usual care and a sleep hygiene brochure. Control arm: Received minimally enhanced usual care (mEUC), which included usual care by their primary care provider (may include pharmacotherapy) and a sleep hygiene brochure.
rows.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index Score (ISI), Quality of Life Score (Short-Form 12 [SF-12], Mental Component Score), Daytime Sleepiness- Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS), Satisfaction With Care Patient Assessment of Chronic Illness Care (PACIC), Sleep Quality Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI), Self-reported Wake After Sleep Onset (14-day sleep diary), Actigraphy Assessed Wake After Sleep Onset (14-days of wrist-actigraphy), Self-reported Sleep Onset Latency (14-day sleep diary).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Change in Mean Insomnia Severity Index Score (ISI) at 9 weeks: CBT-I -6.7 (95% CI -15.4 to 2.0), mEUC -3.9 (95% CI -12.5 to 4.7); at 6 months: CBT-I -5.7, mEUC -1.6. Change in Mean SF-12 MCS at 9 weeks: CBT-I 10.8, mEUC 0.8. Change in Mean ESS at 9 weeks: CBT-I -2.3, mEUC 0.5. Change in Mean PACIC at 9 weeks: CBT-I 17.4, mEUC -8.2. Change in Mean PSQI at 9 weeks: CBT-I -1.0, mEUC -0.7. Change in Mean Actigraphy Assessed Wake After Sleep Onset at 9 weeks: CBT-I -2.5 mins, mEUC -4.8 mins. Change in Mean Self-reported Sleep Onset Latency at 9 weeks: CBT-I -53.3 mins, mEUC -16.9 mins. Self-reported Wake After Sleep Onset was not analyzed as data was not collected.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome is assessed as a change from baseline to 9-week postrandomisation.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Participants are randomised 1:1 to receive the prescription digital therapeutic (PDT) or not receive it.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One arm receives a prescription digital therapeutic (PDT) (PEAR-003b, Somryst) providing mobile-delivered CBT-I. The control arm does not receive the PDT. All participants are provided with a linked Fitbit (Inspire 2) to track activity.
rows.collected_data: Insomnia severity index score, healthcare utilisation, health utility scores, clinical outcomes, sleep outcomes (measured with sleep diaries), depressive symptoms, daytime sleepiness, health status, stress, anxiety, activity (via Fitbit Inspire 2), and engagement with the PDT.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: Assessed at baseline, posttest (9 weeks) and at 6-, 12-, and 18-month follow-ups.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: SHUTi (digital CBT-I intervention) and HealthWatch (attention-matched control internet program)
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: SHUTi is a self-guided, digital CBT-I intervention. HealthWatch is an attention-matched control internet program.
rows.collected_data: Online sleep diaries, sleep-onset latency (SOL), wake after sleep onset (WASO), sleep efficiency (SE), number of awakenings, sleep quality, and total sleep time (TST).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the SHUTi condition had greater improvements at posttest compared with control for: SOL, WASO, SE, number of awakenings, and sleep quality. These improvements were sustained at every follow-up (p < .02 for all outcomes except TST, in which statistically significant increases were observed only at 12- and 18-months).

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective study of the MAUDE database through April 30, 2025.
rows.collected_data: Event type, patient demographics, outcomes, and product details.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Four reports were initially identified. Two were excluded. Of the remaining two, one report involved Somryst for insomnia prescribed to a patient with a seizure disorder (a known contraindication) and resulted in disability. The other involved EndeavorRx, which reported perceived ineffectiveness without clinical harm. No adverse events were identified for the remaining 11 PDTs.

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: 9-week intervention period with assessments at baseline, before completing treatment cores, immediately post-intervention, and at 6-month and 1-year follow-ups.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with 1565 adults receiving the intervention.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm receiving an FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutic (PDT; Somryst®) delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia via six interactive treatment cores and daily sleep diaries.
rows.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index [ISI], 8-item Patient Health Questionnaire [PHQ-8], Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 scale [GAD-7], daily sleep diaries, and treatment adherence/completion rates.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Of 1565 adults, 908 (58.0%) were adherent and 733 (46.8%) completed all 6 cores. For those completing assessments for all 6 cores, ISI decreased from 18.8 to 9.9 (P <.001). ISI scores remained significantly lower than baseline at immediate post-intervention (11.0), 6-month (11.6), and 1-year follow-ups (12.2) (P <.001). PHQ-8 and GAD-7 scores also showed significant decreases at all measured timepoints from baseline (P <.001).

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: A pre/post analysis was conducted comparing healthcare resource utilization 2 years pre-index to 2 years post-index.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single cohort of 248 patients receiving the intervention (pre/post analysis, no separate control group).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Patients with self-identified sleep problems who successfully initiated Somryst (previously called SHUTi), a digital therapeutic delivering cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I).
rows.collected_data: Insomnia severity index (ISI) score, healthcare resource utilization (HCRU) including emergency department visits, hospitalizations, hospital outpatient visits, ambulatory surgical center visits, and office visits, sleep aid medication use, number of prescriptions, and healthcare costs.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: After 9 weeks, mean ISI score declined by 37.2% (19.1 vs 12.0); 58.8% achieved ISI responder status (improvement >=7; NNT: 1.7), and 26.6% achieved insomnia remission (ISI <8; NNT: 3.8). After 2-year follow-up, emergency department visits were reduced by 53% (IRR: 0.47; 95% CI 0.27, 0.82; P=0.008). Non-significant reductions were observed for hospitalizations (-21%; P=0.389) and hospital outpatient visits (-13%; P=0.315). Ambulatory surgical center visits and office visits slightly increased (2%). Patients treated with sleep aid medications dropped 18.5% (52.4% pre-index vs 42.7% post-index). Average prescriptions decreased from 3.98 to 3.73 (P=0.552). Total two-year cost reduction was $510,678 (-$2059 per patient).

rows.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
rows.duration_additional_info: The program had an average duration of 10 weeks, with the primary outcome assessed from baseline to program-end.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 326 participants started the program (264 completed). No control group was mentioned (prospective cohort study).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received a remote digital care program (DCP) combining education and pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) with real-time biofeedback, managed asynchronously by a physical therapist.
rows.collected_data: Urinary Impact Questionnaire-short form (UIQ-7), impact of pelvic conditions (PFIQ-7), depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), productivity impairment (WPAI), intention to seek additional healthcare, engagement, and satisfaction.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Of the 326 participants who started, 264 (81.0%) completed the intervention. Significant improvement on UIQ-7 (8.8, 95%CI 4.7; 12.9, p < 0.001) was observed, corresponding to a response rate of 57.3%. Improvements were also seen in all other outcomes, along with high satisfaction (8.9/10, SD 1.8). The study demonstrated the feasibility and safety of the completely remote DCP.

rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Smartphone app ViViRA Neck; Control group: Standard of care (physiotherapy)

Control group undergoes physiotherapy max. once a week as standard of care.
rows.collected_data: Clinical disease markers: BASMI (mobility), BASFI (function), BASDAI, ASDAS, and ASAS 20 (disease activity); physical activity evaluated by activity sensor; movement and gait analysis with sensors; BSA, WPAI, PSQI, ASqL, SF-36, HAQ, PAIN, TSK, IPAQ, and PAHCO; clinical routine including serology blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation), biochemical markers, physical examination, and bone resorption markers (CTX, osteocalcin, BAP); hand strength.

rows.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome measured after 2, 6 and 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app. Control: Conventional physiotherapy.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Execution of exercises and educations according to guidelines, which are digitally adapted to the individual performance of the patient. Arm 2 (Control): Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists analogue to the agreements with the German statutory health insurance system.
rows.collected_data: Pain reduction measured by VNRS (Visual Numeric Rating Scale) after 2, 6 and 12 weeks; Function improvement of mobility.

rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received ViViRA DHA; Control group received standard physiotherapy.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the digital health application (DHA) ViViRA for home-based physiotherapy, whereas the control group received standard physiotherapy.
rows.collected_data: Pain (verbal rating scale [VRS], PAIN-Detect), quality of life (SF-36), and mobility (BASMI).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline: 1.1 [range 0.7-1.5]; follow-up: 1.0 [range 0.5-1.4]; p = 0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline: 1.5 [range 1.1-1.9]; follow-up: 1.8 [range 1.4-2.2]; p = 0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level at week 3.5 ± 2.8) than did the control group (VRS pain level at week 4.5 ± 2) after 12 weeks.

rows.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise for 6 months. Assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention groups: ViViRA (with personal coaching) and Kaia Health (with AI-based coaching). Control group: ViViRA (without coaching).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Personal coaching (ViViRA) consisted of a one-time, 30-min online coaching session prior to using the digital health application (DHA). AI coaching (Kaia Health) consisted of video-based AI integrated into the DHA to provide movement guidance during each session. The control group used the ViViRA DHA without any coaching.
rows.collected_data: Sociodemographic data, questionnaires, mobility (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index [BASMI]), pain intensity (PainDETECT for neuropathic pain), Physical Activity-related Health Competence (PAHCO), and adherence (frequency of DHA use).
rows.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed a significant improvement in mobility (BL-3 month: mean BASMI change -0.6 to -0.7; all p < 0.001). Pain intensity decreased substantially in all arms (BL-6 month: mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points; all p <= 0.006). PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (BL-6 month: +1.02, p = 0.013) but did not exceed the other interventions in a direct comparison. Overall, none of the coaching strategies showed significant superiority over the stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months.

rows.duration_additional_info: The digital home exercise program lasted 12 weeks; physiotherapy included 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 108 participants; Control group: 105 participants
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: 12-week stand-alone digital home exercise program including 4 exercises daily. Control: standard of care physiotherapy including 6 to 12 sessions, depending on the severity of symptoms.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity assessed using a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS); clinical relevance of pain reduction (thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the VNRS and a pain reduction of at least 30%); retention rates.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: At 12 weeks, the mean difference in pain scores between the 2 groups was -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. The intervention group achieved a clinically relevant reduction in pain (mean change -3.35 [SD 2.05] points or -53.1% [SD 29.5]). The control group did not reach clinical relevance (mean change -0.91 [SD 1.5] points or -14.6% [SD 25.3]). Retention rates were 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group. The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity compared with standard care.

rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with intra-individual control (the control is the preliminary/historical findings of the same patient).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app (In-Home Therapeutic Training Program). Patients watch and follow video exercise demonstrations, read instructions, and input information regarding their wellbeing and configuration needs.
rows.collected_data: Pain using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS) at baseline and after 12 weeks; descriptive variables in the form of a series of functional scores (proprietary functional test).

rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective data collection conducted between 15 June 2021 and 22 June 2021 for patients using the app from 01/01/2020.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study using intraindividual control (the control is the pre-finding/historical finding of the same patient).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app ('Therapeutic Training at Home' digital application on smartphones and tablets, involving video exercise demonstrations, instructions, and a feedback mechanism).
rows.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Pain assessed via a validated verbal-numerical rating scale (VNRS) after 12 weeks. Secondary Outcome: Proprietary functional test / functional scores. Other data: information about patient well-being and configuration needs.

rows.duration_additional_info: Retrospective observational study with intra-individual pre-post comparison.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study of patients using the digital therapeutic 'Vivira' (N = 7842).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intra-individual pre-post comparison of patients with non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity (verbal-numerical rating scale, VNRS); Physical level of activity (mobility, strength, and coordination on a discrete scale); Patient characteristics for treatment adherence.

rows.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks throughout the observation period.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 3629 patients using the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira'. No distinct control group was mentioned (time-series and matched-pair analysis).
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira' for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain score (assessed with a verbal numerical rating scale) and self-reported function scores.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity were observed after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (T3628=5308; P<.001). The changes were within the range of a clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive yet more variable response across the pain areas (back, hip, and knee).

rows.duration_additional_info: The intended duration of the program and assessment of pain score changes was 12 weeks.
rows.intervention_and_control_group: 2517 participants using Vivira, a smartphone-based program for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. The study used an incomplete matched block design.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received Vivira, a smartphone-based digital home exercise program for back, hip, and knee pain.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores (measured on a scale out of 10), self-reported functional scores (strength, mobility, and coordination scores), and adherence rates.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19 out of 10 (SD 1.96) to an average of 3.35 out of 10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores, whereas participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in their coordination scores. A high yet expected attrition rate was observed across all pain areas and durations.

rows.intervention_and_control_group: High use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), and sub-LU use profiles of a digital home exercise program.
rows.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Patients were categorized into four use profiles based on their adherence to a digital home exercise program: high use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), or sub-LU.
rows.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensities; achievement of clinically relevant pain improvement.
rows.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles show a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement of self-reported pain intensities. Additionally, the achievement of any higher use level is associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement.

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intent-to-treat analyses showed significantly lower ADHD symptom severity in the intervention group at 3 months (baseline-adjusted mean difference = -5.0 points; d = 0.85, p < .001). Significant improvements were also observed across all secondary outcomes, and effects remained stable at 6 months. Responder analyses confirmed clinical relevance. Subgroup analyses demonstrated consistent effects across sex, medication use, psychotherapy status, and treatment changes. No adverse events related to attexis were reported.

The control group receives access to the same treatment after a waiting period of 10 weeks.

studies.duration_additional_info: Data were collected at baseline, 9 weeks and 18 weeks. The control group also completed questionnaires 9 weeks after completing the program. Both groups completed questionnaires 6 months after their respective program usage termination.

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: Deprexis® program consisting of ten interactive modules, plus one introductory and one summary module, completed over nine weeks. Control: Waitlist control group that did not receive access to the program initially (delayed treatment).
studies.collected_data: Depressive symptom level measured with the German version of the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI); Work and Social Adjustment Scale (German translation); Subjective usefulness of the program measured by individually designed items.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: 8-week course of Deprexis
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Deprexis intervention and waitlist control
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received either an 8-week course of the Internet intervention Deprexis or were assigned to a waitlist.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depression Symptoms (QIDS), assessing specific symptoms such as sadness, indecision, early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, agitation, and somatic symptoms.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Deprexis directly improved the symptoms of sadness and indecision. Changes in these symptoms were associated with a change in early insomnia, middle insomnia, self-dislike, fatigue, anhedonia, suicidality, slowness, and agitation. All of these symptoms (except for agitation and early insomnia) showed decreases with Deprexis compared with a waitlist after correcting for multiple comparisons. Six additional symptoms, particularly somatic symptoms, were not impacted by Deprexis compared with a waitlist. The efficacy of Deprexis was due to its direct impact on sadness and indecision.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: 12-week period of treatment. No fixed time frame for inpatient treatment duration; post-discharge patients were followed by structured telephone interviews up to the 12-week study endpoint. Measures were carried out at study entry and every 2 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Treatment-as-usual (TAU, N=33) and TAU plus guided deprexis (TAU-PLUS, N=36)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Control arm received treatment-as-usual (TAU). Intervention arm received TAU plus guided deprexis (TAU-PLUS). Both groups received comparable prescribed antidepressant medication.
studies.collected_data: Beck-Depression-Inventory-II (BDI-II), Hamilton-Depression-Scale, Clinical-Global-Impression-Severity, WHO-Well-Being-Index, Helping-Alliance-Questionnaire, working alliance with deprexis, inpatient treatment duration, daily activity, and utilization of post-hospital care after discharge.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At week 12, BDI-II scores showed significant between-group differences favoring TAU-PLUS (p=.03, d=-.73, 95% CI -1.4 to .06). TAU-PLUS patients showed greater daily activity (p=.04, d=.70, 95% CI -.03 to 1.38) and were discharged significantly earlier (p=.003). Post-discharge, TAU-PLUS reported lower rates of post-hospital care (p=.01) and re-admissions (p=.04). Secondary outcome measures (including therapist alliance) were not significantly different between groups at endpoint. Working-alliance with deprexis significantly predicted MDD-improvement and wellbeing.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: 12-week online intervention. Secondary outcomes assessed at baseline, post-treatment, and 3-months' and 9-months' follow-up. Primary outcome (costs) evaluated for the study year.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (deprexis) and a control condition
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to either a 12-week online intervention (deprexis) or a control condition.
studies.collected_data: Statutory health insurance costs (estimated using health insurers' administrative data); depression severity, health-related quality of life, and impairment in functioning (assessed via patient self-report).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Total costs of statutory health insurance decreased by 32% from 3139€ per year at baseline to 2119€ in the study year in the intervention group, compared to a 13% mean reduction in the control group (changes differed significantly). The intervention group also showed a significantly greater reduction in depression severity and impairment in functioning, and a significantly greater increase in health-related quality of life compared to the control group.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The intervention group received immediate access to the program for 90 days (approximately 3 months), while the control group received delayed access after 8 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: n=94; Control: n=95
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention condition: treatment as usual plus immediate access to Deprexis for 90 days. Control condition: treatment as usual and delayed access to Deprexis, after 8 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), semistructured interview, Clinical Outcome in Routine Evaluation-Outcome Measurement (CORE-OM), perceived self-efficacy measure, login frequency, dropout rates, deterioration rates, demographic and clinical variables, and satisfaction with Deprexis.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the immediate access group logged in an average of 14.81 (SD 12.16) times. The intervention group improved significantly more than the delayed access control group on the primary depression measure (PHQ-9; Cohen d=0.80; P<.001), general psychological state (CORE-OM; Cohen d=0.82; P<.001), and perceived self-efficacy (Cohen d=0.63; P<.001). Remission was achieved by 21% (20/94) of intervention participants compared with 7% (7/95) in the control group (P<.001). Deterioration rates were lower in the immediate access group. The dropout rate was high, but no differences in demographic and clinical variables were found. Participants reported a medium to high level of satisfaction.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: 8-week course of treatment immediately or after an 8-week delay (waitlist control).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (immediate treatment) n = 285; Control (waitlist) n = 91
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an 8-week course of Deprexis (an Internet treatment for depression with relatively minimal support) immediately. The control group was a waitlist control that received the treatment after an 8-week delay.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms-Self-Report (QIDS-SR), self-reported symptoms of depression, interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, depression-related disability, symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses indicated that treatment was associated with greater reduction in self-reported symptoms of depression (effect size d = .80) and 12 times greater likelihood of experiencing at least 50% symptom improvement compared with waitlist control. Similar effects were observed for interviewer-rated depression symptoms, well-being, and depression-related disability. Treatment effects for symptoms of social anxiety, panic, and traumatic intrusions were relatively small.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Working alliance was assessed after 6 and 12 weeks. There was a high dropout rate at the 6-month follow-up.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: psychotherapy plus a web-based depression program ('deprexis'); Control: regular psychotherapy
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to one of two active treatment conditions: regular psychotherapy alone or regular psychotherapy combined with a web-based depression program ('deprexis').
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms (primary outcome); anxiety symptoms, somatic symptoms, and quality of life (secondary outcomes); working alliance.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The combination of psychotherapy with the web-based program ('deprexis') was more effective than psychotherapy alone at 12 weeks. There were medium between-group effect sizes on primary depressive symptoms (Cohen's d = .51) and small to medium between-group effect sizes on secondary outcomes (Cohen's d = .07-.55). No negative side effects were observed in the blended format, such as a lower working alliance compared to psychotherapy alone.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Measurements were taken at pre-treatment, post-treatment (9 weeks), and at a 6-month follow-up.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: N = 320; Control: N = 76
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received 9 weeks of immediate-program-access to the Deprexis program as an add-on to treatment-as-usual. The control group received a 9-week delayed-access plus treatment-as-usual condition.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) to measure depression (primary outcome measure); Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSA) to measure social functioning (secondary outcome measure).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Of 396 participants, 216 (55%) completed the post-measurement 9 weeks later. Available case analyses revealed a significant reduction in depression severity (BDI), Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.94), and significant improvement in social functioning (WSA), Cohen's d = .64 (CI 95% = 0.33 - 0.95). These improvements were maintained at 6-month follow-up. Intention-to-treat analyses confirmed significant effects on depression and social functioning improvements (BDI: Cohen's d = .30, CI 95% = 0.05 - 0.55; WSA: Cohen's d = .36, CI 95% = 0.10 - 0.61). A much higher percentage of patients in the intervention group experienced a significant reduction of depression symptoms (BDI: odds ratio [OR] = 6.8, CI 95% = 2.90 - 18.19) and recovered more often (OR = 17.3, 95% CI 2.3 - 130). More than 80% of the users felt subjectively that the program had been helpful.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome is assessed at 3 months post randomization. All outcomes are assessed again at a secondary endpoint 6 months post randomization.

studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI-II) for depressive symptoms, indicators of anxiety, somatic symptoms, quality of life, working alliance, and feasibility/acceptability of the treatment condition.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were given access to Deprexis for 12 weeks. Randomization occurred after the first 2 weeks. TILS was administered once a week for 4 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Deprexis + TILS; Control: Deprexis + sham/placebo (40 participants total randomized between the two groups)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: All participants received Deprexis (internet-based CBT). The intervention group received TILS to the right forehead once a week for 4 weeks, while the control group received a sham/placebo.
studies.collected_data: Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptomatology-Self-Report (QIDS-SR) scores; adverse effects.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: There was a 43% reduction in QIDS-SR scores in the sham group from baseline to week 12, compared to a 56% reduction in the TILS group (an additional 30% reduction, [56-43]/43 = 30%). Participants receiving TILS showed a significantly greater reduction of depressive symptoms than those receiving sham/placebo. No adverse effects were reported.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Re-assessment occurred after 8 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group and wait-list control group
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an online-intervention for depression ('Deprexis'). The control group was a wait-list control group.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire - 9 (PHQ-9) (primary outcome), Pathological Gambling Adaptation of Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (PG-YBOCS), and South Oaks Gambling Screen (SOGS).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: ITT analyses showed that the intervention led to a significant reduction in depressive symptoms as well as gambling-related symptoms compared to the control group, with moderate to strong effect sizes. PP analyses failed to yield significant results due to high rates of non-completion and limited statistical power. Moderator analyses indicated that Deprexis was particularly beneficial in reducing problematic gambling for those scoring high on baseline gambling-related symptoms and for those who gamble due to loneliness.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Intermediary and long-term assessments were conducted over 36 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Internet intervention for depression (Deprexis) compared with care as usual.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One arm received an Internet intervention for depression (Deprexis) and the control arm received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Depressive symptoms (including worthlessness, fatigue, depressed mood, and concentration) and quality of life aspects (including overall impairment through emotional problems and activity level).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Item-level analysis showed that scale-level effects can be explained by small item-level effects on most depressive symptoms at all points of assessment. Higher scores on these items at baseline predicted overall symptom reduction throughout the whole assessment period. Deprexis directly affected certain symptoms of depression (e.g., worthlessness and fatigue) and certain aspects of the quality of life (e.g., overall impairment through emotional problems), while other domains were affected indirectly (e.g., depressed mood and concentration as well as activity level).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed on patients' self-report at baseline, three months, and six months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (deprexis + care as usual) and Control group (care as usual)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received a 12-week web-based cognitive behavioral intervention ('deprexis') in addition to care as usual. Control group received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Health-related resource use, use of medication, incapacity to work, and direct health care costs (assessed via patients' self-report).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Total direct health care costs decreased in both groups during the study period, but changes from baseline did not significantly differ between study groups. Costs for psychotherapeutic treatment decreased in the intervention group (-16.8% (€80)) whereas they increased in the control group (+14.7% (€60)) (tdf=685=2.57; p=0.008).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Patients were allocated to their respective groups for a 9-week period.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (n=45 allocated, 35 completed) and waitlist control group (n=45 allocated, 36 completed)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received Deprexis, a fully automated, internet-based, cognitive behavioural therapy programme. The control group was a waitlist control.
studies.collected_data: Beck Depression Inventory (BDI); adverse events (suicidal ideation).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: 71 patients completed the trial. During the intervention, BDI scores decreased in the Deprexis group and increased in the control group, yielding a positive effect of Deprexis relative to the waitlist group (mean group difference -4.02 points [95% CI -7.26 to -0.79], p=0.015, effect size d=0.53). Worsening of depressive symptoms from below to above the clinical cutoff (BDI >13) occurred in 3 (7%) of 45 patients in the control group and 0 patients in the Deprexis group. There were no adverse events with respect to new occurrence of suicidal ideation.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Symptoms were assessed pre and post intervention at 12 weeks.
studies.study_size_participants: 509 in the intervention group (total study size not specified in the abstract)
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Care as usual (CAU) plus the Internet intervention Deprexis (n=509); Control: Care as usual (CAU)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group received CAU plus the Internet intervention Deprexis. Participants with moderate symptom severity at baseline also received e-mail support (guidance). Control group received care as usual (CAU).
studies.collected_data: Depression symptoms, patient characteristics, self-rating screening for mental disorders, attitudes towards online interventions, quality of life, and adherence (usage time and number of sessions).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Adherence was good with an average of 7 hours of usage time and 8 sessions. Patient characteristics (age, sex, depressive symptoms, and confidence in the program's effectiveness) predicted a higher number of sessions (explaining 15-25% of variance). Older age (β = .16) and higher depressive symptoms (β = .15) were associated with higher usage duration. Higher adherence predicted greater reduction in depressive symptoms over 12 weeks (number of sessions: β = .13, usage duration: β = .14), though this was mostly explained by receiving guidance (β = .27 and .26). Overall, older age was associated with adherence, and adherence was positively associated with outcome, although the effects were small.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary endpoint assessed at 12 weeks post-test. Secondary outcomes assessed at 12 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: n=509; Control group: n=504
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received an online intervention (Deprexis) in addition to care as usual (CAU). The control group received CAU alone.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression, Quick Inventory of Depressive Symptoms, and sociodemographic variables (sex, age, educational attainment, employment status, relationship status, and lifetime frequency of episodes).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Between-group differences were observed in post-test scores, indicating the effectiveness of Deprexis. The effects of the intervention were demonstrated across all subgroups. After exploring moderating effects based on linear regression models, none of the selected sociodemographic variables was found to be moderating treatment outcomes. The findings suggest that Deprexis is equally beneficial to a wide range of people, and participant characteristics were not associated with treatment benefits.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention (Deprexis + care-as-usual): n = 509; Control (care-as-usual): n = 504
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group accessed the Internet intervention Deprexis adjunctively to care-as-usual. The control group had access to care-as-usual.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) scores; Web Screening Questionnaire (assessing anxiety-related comorbidities including generalized anxiety disorder, social phobia, panic disorder, agoraphobia, panic disorder with agoraphobia, specific phobia, posttraumatic stress disorder, and obsessive-compulsive disorder).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Out of 12 potential moderators examined, only social phobia moderated the intervention effect on depression outcome in favor of Deprexis (p < .05 for both continuous PHQ-9 scores and clinically important PHQ-9 changes). However, the moderating effects did not reach small effect sizes, accounted for less than 1% of the variance in change in depressive symptoms, and showed limited reproducibility in randomly selected split halves.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at baseline and posttreatment.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The study used data from a trial examining a guided Internet intervention (deprexis) for patients with mild to moderate depression.
studies.collected_data: Working Alliance Inventory adapted for guided Internet interventions (WAI-I), Patient Satisfaction Questionnaire (ZUF-8), and Attitudes toward Psychological Online-Interventions Questionnaire (APOI).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The results suggested a two-factor solution for the WAI-I, with a very good model fit, evidence of factor independency, adequate internal consistency, and external validity for the complete scale and the sub-scales. The WAI-I was shown to be a reliable and valid instrument to capture alliance in guided Internet interventions.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Early change was assessed in the first four weeks of the intervention. Long-term outcome (onset of remission) was tracked over a 36-month (3-year) follow-up period.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants used the web-based intervention 'deprexis' for mild to moderate depressive symptoms.
studies.collected_data: Baseline parameters/variables, early change in depressive symptoms (assessed in the first four weeks), and onset of remission over 36 months.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Early change class was a significant predictor of remission over 36 months. Compared to early deterioration after screening, both early response after registration and after screening were associated with a higher likelihood of remission. In sensitivity and secondary analyses, only change class consistently emerged as a predictor of long-term outcome. Those showing early improvement had a higher likelihood of long-term remission.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Participants completed the PHQ-9 biweekly during the 12-week treatment.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Participants were randomized to a 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis); control group details are not specified in the abstract.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: 12-week Internet-based intervention (deprexis) for patients with mild-to-moderate depression symptoms.
studies.collected_data: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9) assessed biweekly during treatment; dropout rates/probability; reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Four change-dropout patterns were identified: C3 (N=134) had high impairment, improvement, and low dropout probability, with the highest rate of reliable change at 6- and 12-month follow-up. C2 (N=32) had high impairment, deterioration, and high dropout probability. C1 (N=198) had low impairment, improvement, and high dropout probability. C4 (N=119) had high impairment, no change, and low dropout probability. The study concluded that both deterioration and rapid improvement (perceived 'good enough' dosage) may lead to dropout.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline, 3 months (post-assessment) and 6 months (follow-up).
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 12-week Psychological online intervention (Deprexis); Control: Care as usual (CAU)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to either a 12-week psychological online intervention (Deprexis) or care as usual (CAU).
studies.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9), intervention use (frequency and duration), and attitudes towards psychological online interventions (POIs).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Compared to Millennials, Boomers used the intervention significantly more often (d = 0.45) and for a longer duration (d = 0.46), and endorsed more positive attitudes towards POIs (d = 0.14). There was no significant Age Group by Intervention Group interaction for change in PHQ-9. The post-assessment between-group effect size (intervention vs. CAU control) for Millennials and Boomers were d = 0.26 and d = 0.39, respectively, and were stable at follow-up (d = 0.37 and d = 0.39). The POI was superior to CAU and was comparably effective among Millennials and Baby Boomers.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (Deprexis) and Control group (care as usual)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the internet-based intervention Deprexis, while the control group received care as usual. All participants in both conditions were permitted to use any type of treatment.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) to assess nine depressive symptoms and overall depression severity.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Seven of the nine considered symptoms showed larger improvements in the intervention condition relative to care as usual (effect sizes ranging from 0.15 to 0.31). No significant differences were found for the two other symptoms. Network estimation suggested that four of the seven symptom-specific effects were direct, whereas three were indirect. Exploratory analyses showed the intervention was more effective in improving overall depression severity for participants with higher scores on the four symptoms directly affected by the intervention.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: 3 months intervention period
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (deprexis program) and a control group (care as usual)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the depression-focused Internet intervention program 'deprexis', while the control group received care as usual.
studies.collected_data: Attitudes toward Internet interventions (assessed pre to post over a 3 months intervention period), efficacy of the program, and usage time.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Positive initial attitudes toward Internet interventions were associated with greater efficacy (η2p = .014) independent of usage time, whereas a negative attitude (perceived lack of personal contact) was associated with reduced efficacy (η2p = .012). Users' attitudes changed during the trial, and both the magnitude and direction of attitude change were associated with the efficacy of the program over time (η2p = .030).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants were randomized to either care as usual alone (CAU) or a 12-week Internet intervention (Deprexis) adjunctive to usual care.
studies.collected_data: Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9) for self-rated depression severity.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Changes in PHQ-9 from baseline differed significantly between groups (t825 = 6.12, p < 0.001). The post-assessment between-group effect size in favour of the intervention was d = 0.39 (95% CI: 0.13-0.64) and was stable at follow-up with d = 0.32 (95% CI: 0.06-0.69). The rate of participants experiencing at least minimally clinically important PHQ-9 change at post-assessment was higher in the intervention group (35.6% vs. 20.2%) with a number needed to treat of 7 (95% CI: 5-10).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

studies.collected_data: Symptoms of depression, therapeutic progress, mental health-related quality of life, physical health-related quality of life, and rates of clinical improvement.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intention-to-treat analyses indicated that combined treatment led to a greater reduction in symptoms of depression (effect size d = 0.32; p = .002), improved therapeutic progress (d = 0.36; p = .003), and higher mental health-related quality of life (d = 0.34; p = .004). There was no intervention effect on physical health-related quality of life. The same pattern was found at 6-month follow-up, and adjunctive treatment also resulted in increased rates of clinical improvement.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: 12-week internet intervention with regular assessments over a twelve-month period.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group (internet intervention plus usual care) and Control group (care as usual alone). Total N = 1013.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care. Control arm received care as usual alone.
studies.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (PHQ-9), remission rates, and antidepressant medication usage.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Remission rates over time were significantly higher in the intervention group (HR 1.31; p = 0.009). The intervention was more effective in the subgroup not taking antidepressant medication (HR 1.88; p < 0.001). PHQ-change from baseline was greater in the intervention group (p < 0.001) with the between-group effect gradually decreasing from d = 0.36 at three months to d = 0.13 at twelve months (group by time interaction: p < 0.001).

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: The internet intervention lasted 12 weeks. The primary outcome measure was assessed at 3 months and 6 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Care-as-usual alone (control) and a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care (intervention).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Control arm received care-as-usual alone. Intervention arm received a 12-week internet intervention (Deprexis) plus usual care.
studies.collected_data: Self-rated depression severity (Patient Health Questionnaire-9); demographic and clinical parameters; attitudes towards internet interventions (Attitudes towards Psychological Online Interventions Questionnaire).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The recruitment source was only associated with very few of the examined demographic and clinical characteristics. Compared with participants recruited from clinical sources, participants recruited through insurance companies were more likely to be employed. Clinically recruited participants were as severely affected as those from other recruitment sources but more sceptical of internet interventions. The effectiveness of the intervention was not differentially associated with recruitment source (treatment by recruitment source interaction=0.28, p=0.84). The results support the hypothesis that the intervention is effective across different recruitment sources including clinical settings.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at 3 months (T1) and 6 months (T2).

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Intent-to-treat (ITT) analyses revealed significant improvements in fatigue in the intervention group compared to the control group after 3 months (Cohen’s d: 0.29) and 6 months (Cohen’s d: 0.31). Depressive symptoms decreased significantly over the observation period: svMADRS scores decreased from 29.72 (SD=10.03) to 15.73 (SD=9.74) (Cohen's d=1.42, 95% CI 0.08-2.76) and PHQ-9 scores decreased from 15.20 (SD=5.03) to 8.77 (SD=5.03) (d=1.29, 95% CI 0.60-1.97).

studies.key_outcomes_findings: The interventional group showed positive outcomes on the KOOS Pain and Symptoms subscale compared to the standard of care group. Statistically significant pain reduction was seen at t1 (p = 0.0005), t2 (p = 0.0068) and t4 (approximately 6 weeks after surgery; p = 0.01). Significant reduction of symptoms at t1 (p = 0.0001), t2 (p = 0.0036) and t6 (p = 0.0377) were recognized. These findings are further supported by the significant benefit of the pain NRS at t4 and positive tendencies at t3, t5 and t6. The KOOS subscales quality of life (p = 0.0066), activities of daily living (p = 0.0009) and Sport (p = 0.0484) showed significant effects before surgery.

studies.collected_data: Osteoarthritis-specific pain (Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score [KOOS]), all other KOOS subscales, general health-related quality of life (Veterans RAND 12-item Health Survey), psychological measures (e.g., exercise self-efficacy), performance measures (strength and postural control), adherence, and safety.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Statistically significant effects in favor of the IG for pain reduction (P<.001; ES=0.76), improvements in physical function (P<.001; ES=0.64), symptoms (P=.01; ES=0.53), sport and recreation activities (P=.02; ES=0.47), knee-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.76), and the physical component of general health-related quality of life (P<.001; ES=0.74). Mean differences ranged from 6.0 to 13.2 points. No effects were found for psychological and performance measures. Participants adhered to 92.5% (899/972) of all scheduled exercise sessions.

studies.key_outcomes_findings: At 8- and 16-weeks post-randomisation, large treatment effects (d = 0.87–1.08) indicated robust reductions (-3.70 and -2.97, respectively; p ≤ 0.003) in insomnia severity in the digital cognitive behavioural therapy arm, relative to digital sleep monitoring. studies.key_outcomes_findings: At posttreatment (8 weeks), dCBT-I was associated with a large reduction in the ISI (adjusted difference = -7.60) compared to the waitlist control (d = -2.08). studies.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed a significant improvement in mobility (BL-3 month: mean BASMI change -0.6 to -0.7; all p < 0.001). Pain intensity decreased substantially in all arms (BL-6 month: mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points; all p <= 0.006). PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (BL-6 month: +1.02, p = 0.013) but did not exceed the other interventions in a direct comparison. Overall, none of the coaching strategies showed significant superiority over the stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months.

studies.risk_class: Risk Class I according to MDR

The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity compared with standard care.

studies.key_outcomes_findings: Initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19 out of 10 (SD 1.96) to an average of 3.35 out of 10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores, whereas participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in their coordination scores.
