studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm: Received a self-guided digital program of CBT-I (Somryst) culturally adapted for Spanish-language speakers, consisting of six cores over 9 weeks, plus usual care and a sleep hygiene brochure. Control arm: Received minimally enhanced usual care (mEUC), which included usual care by their primary care provider (may include pharmacotherapy) and a sleep hygiene brochure.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index Score (ISI), Quality of Life Score (Short-Form 12 [SF-12], Mental Component Score), Daytime Sleepiness- Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS), Satisfaction With Care Patient Assessment of Chronic Illness Care (PACIC), Sleep Quality Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI), Self-reported Wake After Sleep Onset (14-day sleep diary), Actigraphy Assessed Wake After Sleep Onset (14-days of wrist-actigraphy), Self-reported Sleep Onset Latency (14-day sleep diary).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Change in Mean Insomnia Severity Index Score (ISI) at 9 weeks: CBT-I -6.7 (95% CI -15.4 to 2.0), mEUC -3.9 (95% CI -12.5 to 4.7); at 6 months: CBT-I -5.7, mEUC -1.6. Change in Mean SF-12 MCS at 9 weeks: CBT-I 10.8, mEUC 0.8. Change in Mean ESS at 9 weeks: CBT-I -2.3, mEUC 0.5. Change in Mean PACIC at 9 weeks: CBT-I 17.4, mEUC -8.2. Change in Mean PSQI at 9 weeks: CBT-I -1.0, mEUC -0.7. Change in Mean Actigraphy Assessed Wake After Sleep Onset at 9 weeks: CBT-I -2.5 mins, mEUC -4.8 mins. Change in Mean Self-reported Sleep Onset Latency at 9 weeks: CBT-I -53.3 mins, mEUC -16.9 mins. Self-reported Wake After Sleep Onset was not analyzed as data was not collected.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome is assessed as a change from baseline to 9-week postrandomisation.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Participants are randomised 1:1 to receive the prescription digital therapeutic (PDT) or not receive it.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: One arm receives a prescription digital therapeutic (PDT) (PEAR-003b, Somryst) providing mobile-delivered CBT-I. The control arm does not receive the PDT. All participants are provided with a linked Fitbit (Inspire 2) to track activity.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia severity index score, healthcare utilisation, health utility scores, clinical outcomes, sleep outcomes (measured with sleep diaries), depressive symptoms, daytime sleepiness, health status, stress, anxiety, activity (via Fitbit Inspire 2), and engagement with the PDT.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: Assessed at baseline, posttest (9 weeks) and at 6-, 12-, and 18-month follow-ups.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: SHUTi (digital CBT-I intervention) and HealthWatch (attention-matched control internet program)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: SHUTi is a self-guided, digital CBT-I intervention. HealthWatch is an attention-matched control internet program.
studies.collected_data: Online sleep diaries, sleep-onset latency (SOL), wake after sleep onset (WASO), sleep efficiency (SE), number of awakenings, sleep quality, and total sleep time (TST).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Participants in the SHUTi condition had greater improvements at posttest compared with control for: SOL, WASO, SE, number of awakenings, and sleep quality. These improvements were sustained at every follow-up (p < .02 for all outcomes except TST, in which statistically significant increases were observed only at 12- and 18-months).

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective study of the MAUDE database through April 30, 2025.
studies.collected_data: Event type, patient demographics, outcomes, and product details.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Four reports were initially identified. Two were excluded. Of the remaining two, one report involved Somryst for insomnia prescribed to a patient with a seizure disorder (a known contraindication) and resulted in disability. The other involved EndeavorRx, which reported perceived ineffectiveness without clinical harm. No adverse events were identified for the remaining 11 PDTs.

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: 9-week intervention period with assessments at baseline, before completing treatment cores, immediately post-intervention, and at 6-month and 1-year follow-ups.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with 1565 adults receiving the intervention.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Single arm receiving an FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutic (PDT; Somryst®) delivering cognitive-behavioral therapy for insomnia via six interactive treatment cores and daily sleep diaries.
studies.collected_data: Insomnia Severity Index [ISI], 8-item Patient Health Questionnaire [PHQ-8], Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 scale [GAD-7], daily sleep diaries, and treatment adherence/completion rates.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Of 1565 adults, 908 (58.0%) were adherent and 733 (46.8%) completed all 6 cores. For those completing assessments for all 6 cores, ISI decreased from 18.8 to 9.9 (P <.001). ISI scores remained significantly lower than baseline at immediate post-intervention (11.0), 6-month (11.6), and 1-year follow-ups (12.2) (P <.001). PHQ-8 and GAD-7 scores also showed significant decreases at all measured timepoints from baseline (P <.001).

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: A pre/post analysis was conducted comparing healthcare resource utilization 2 years pre-index to 2 years post-index.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single cohort of 248 patients receiving the intervention (pre/post analysis, no separate control group).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Patients with self-identified sleep problems who successfully initiated Somryst (previously called SHUTi), a digital therapeutic delivering cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I).
studies.collected_data: Insomnia severity index (ISI) score, healthcare resource utilization (HCRU) including emergency department visits, hospitalizations, hospital outpatient visits, ambulatory surgical center visits, and office visits, sleep aid medication use, number of prescriptions, and healthcare costs.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: After 9 weeks, mean ISI score declined by 37.2% (19.1 vs 12.0); 58.8% achieved ISI responder status (improvement >=7; NNT: 1.7), and 26.6% achieved insomnia remission (ISI <8; NNT: 3.8). After 2-year follow-up, emergency department visits were reduced by 53% (IRR: 0.47; 95% CI 0.27, 0.82; P=0.008). Non-significant reductions were observed for hospitalizations (-21%; P=0.389) and hospital outpatient visits (-13%; P=0.315). Ambulatory surgical center visits and office visits slightly increased (2%). Patients treated with sleep aid medications dropped 18.5% (52.4% pre-index vs 42.7% post-index). Average prescriptions decreased from 3.98 to 3.73 (P=0.552). Total two-year cost reduction was $510,678 (-$2059 per patient).

studies.risk_class: Class II according to FDA
studies.duration_additional_info: The program had an average duration of 10 weeks, with the primary outcome assessed from baseline to program-end.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 326 participants started the program (264 completed). No control group was mentioned (prospective cohort study).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received a remote digital care program (DCP) combining education and pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) with real-time biofeedback, managed asynchronously by a physical therapist.
studies.collected_data: Urinary Impact Questionnaire-short form (UIQ-7), impact of pelvic conditions (PFIQ-7), depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), productivity impairment (WPAI), intention to seek additional healthcare, engagement, and satisfaction.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Of the 326 participants who started, 264 (81.0%) completed the intervention. Significant improvement on UIQ-7 (8.8, 95%CI 4.7; 12.9, p < 0.001) was observed, corresponding to a response rate of 57.3%. Improvements were also seen in all other outcomes, along with high satisfaction (8.9/10, SD 1.8). The study demonstrated the feasibility and safety of the completely remote DCP.

studies.duration_additional_info: Both the intervention and control groups receive their respective treatments over a 12-week period.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: Smartphone app ViViRA Neck; Control group: Standard of care (physiotherapy)
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention arm receives access to the smartphone app ViViRA Neck for 12 weeks. Control arm receives standard of care, defined as at least 1 prescription with 6 sessions of individual physical therapy over 12 weeks.
studies.collected_data: Pain intensity: Visual Analogue Scale (VAS, 0-100); Disability: Neck Disability Index (NDI); Quality of life: Veterans Rand 12-item health survey (VR12).

studies.duration_additional_info: Participants in the intervention group are expected to exercise with the app for 12 weeks, optimally for 20 minutes 3 times a week.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Group 1 (Intervention): ViViRA digital health app. Group 2 (Control): Physiotherapy max. once a week.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention group receives a prescription for the ViViRA digital health app to exercise for 12 weeks (recommended 20 minutes, 3 times a week) and a wristband sensor to measure physical activity. Control group undergoes physiotherapy max. once a week as standard of care.
studies.collected_data: Clinical disease markers: BASMI (mobility), BASFI (function), BASDAI, ASDAS, and ASAS 20 (disease activity); physical activity evaluated by activity sensor; movement and gait analysis with sensors; BSA, WPAI, PSQI, ASqL, SF-36, HAQ, PAIN, TSK, IPAQ, and PAHCO; clinical routine including serology blood tests (CRP, blood sedimentation), biochemical markers, physical examination, and bone resorption markers (CTX, osteocalcin, BAP); hand strength.

studies.duration_additional_info: Primary outcome measured after 2, 6 and 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: Personalized exercise therapy with the Vivira app. Control: Conventional physiotherapy.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1 (Intervention): Execution of exercises and educations according to guidelines, which are digitally adapted to the individual performance of the patient. Arm 2 (Control): Conventional physiotherapy by physiotherapists analogue to the agreements with the German statutory health insurance system.
studies.collected_data: Pain reduction measured by VNRS (Visual Numeric Rating Scale) after 2, 6 and 12 weeks; Function improvement of mobility.

studies.duration_additional_info: Outcomes were assessed at baseline and after 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group received ViViRA DHA; Control group received standard physiotherapy.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: The intervention group received the digital health application (DHA) ViViRA for home-based physiotherapy, whereas the control group received standard physiotherapy.
studies.collected_data: Pain (verbal rating scale [VRS], PAIN-Detect), quality of life (SF-36), and mobility (BASMI).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: The intervention group showed a significant improvement in mobility (average BASMI score: baseline: 1.1 [range 0.7-1.5]; follow-up: 1.0 [range 0.5-1.4]; p = 0.05), whereas the control group showed a significant decrease in mobility (baseline: 1.5 [range 1.1-1.9]; follow-up: 1.8 [range 1.4-2.2]; p = 0.00). The intervention group demonstrated lower pain intensity (VRS pain level at week 3.5 ± 2.8) than did the control group (VRS pain level at week 4.5 ± 2) after 12 weeks.

studies.duration_additional_info: Participants were instructed to exercise for 6 months. Assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention groups: ViViRA (with personal coaching) and Kaia Health (with AI-based coaching). Control group: ViViRA (without coaching).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Personal coaching (ViViRA) consisted of a one-time, 30-min online coaching session prior to using the digital health application (DHA). AI coaching (Kaia Health) consisted of video-based AI integrated into the DHA to provide movement guidance during each session. The control group used the ViViRA DHA without any coaching.
studies.collected_data: Sociodemographic data, questionnaires, mobility (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Metrology Index [BASMI]), pain intensity (PainDETECT for neuropathic pain), Physical Activity-related Health Competence (PAHCO), and adherence (frequency of DHA use).
studies.key_outcomes_findings: All three digital interventions showed a significant improvement in mobility (BL-3 month: mean BASMI change -0.6 to -0.7; all p < 0.001). Pain intensity decreased substantially in all arms (BL-6 month: mean reduction -4.6 to -6.6 points; all p <= 0.006). PAHCO control competence increased over time and reached statistical significance only in the ViViRA + coaching group (BL-6 month: +1.02, p = 0.013) but did not exceed the other interventions in a direct comparison. Overall, none of the coaching strategies showed significant superiority over the stand-alone digital therapy. Adherence was the same in all groups after 3 months.

studies.duration_additional_info: The digital home exercise program lasted 12 weeks; physiotherapy included 6 to 12 sessions depending on symptom severity.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention group: 108 participants; Control group: 105 participants
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intervention: 12-week stand-alone digital home exercise program including 4 exercises daily. Control: standard of care physiotherapy including 6 to 12 sessions, depending on the severity of symptoms.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity assessed using a verbal numerical rating scale (VNRS); clinical relevance of pain reduction (thresholds: improvement of at least 1.4 points on the VNRS and a pain reduction of at least 30%); retention rates.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: At 12 weeks, the mean difference in pain scores between the 2 groups was -2.44 (95% CI -2.92 to -1.95; P<.01) in favor of the intervention group. The intervention group achieved a clinically relevant reduction in pain (mean change -3.35 [SD 2.05] points or -53.1% [SD 29.5]). The control group did not reach clinical relevance (mean change -0.91 [SD 1.5] points or -14.6% [SD 25.3]). Retention rates were 89.9% in the intervention group and 97.3% in the control group. The app-based home exercise program led to a significant and clinically relevant reduction in pain intensity compared with standard care.

studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study with intra-individual control (the control is the preliminary/historical findings of the same patient).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app (In-Home Therapeutic Training Program). Patients watch and follow video exercise demonstrations, read instructions, and input information regarding their wellbeing and configuration needs.
studies.collected_data: Pain using a validated numerical-verbal rating scale (NVRS/VNRS) at baseline and after 12 weeks; descriptive variables in the form of a series of functional scores (proprietary functional test).

studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective data collection conducted between 15 June 2021 and 22 June 2021 for patients using the app from 01/01/2020.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study using intraindividual control (the control is the pre-finding/historical finding of the same patient).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Arm 1: Retrospective recording of treatment using the Vivira app ('Therapeutic Training at Home' digital application on smartphones and tablets, involving video exercise demonstrations, instructions, and a feedback mechanism).
studies.collected_data: Primary Outcome: Pain assessed via a validated verbal-numerical rating scale (VNRS) after 12 weeks. Secondary Outcome: Proprietary functional test / functional scores. Other data: information about patient well-being and configuration needs.

studies.duration_additional_info: Retrospective observational study with intra-individual pre-post comparison.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Single-arm study of patients using the digital therapeutic 'Vivira' (N = 7842).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Intra-individual pre-post comparison of patients with non-specific and degenerative musculoskeletal conditions undergoing a twelve-week individualized home exercise program.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensity (verbal-numerical rating scale, VNRS); Physical level of activity (mobility, strength, and coordination on a discrete scale); Patient characteristics for treatment adherence.

studies.duration_additional_info: Assessments were conducted at 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks throughout the observation period.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: Intervention: 3629 patients using the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira'. No distinct control group was mentioned (time-series and matched-pair analysis).
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received the smartphone-based home exercise program 'Vivira' for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain score (assessed with a verbal numerical rating scale) and self-reported function scores.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Significant reductions in self-reported pain intensity were observed after 2, 4, 8, and 12 weeks (T3628=5308; P<.001). The changes were within the range of a clinically relevant improvement. Function scores showed a generally positive yet more variable response across the pain areas (back, hip, and knee).

studies.duration_additional_info: The intended duration of the program and assessment of pain score changes was 12 weeks.
studies.intervention_and_control_group: 2517 participants using Vivira, a smartphone-based program for unspecific and degenerative pain in the back, hip, and knee. The study used an incomplete matched block design.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Participants received Vivira, a smartphone-based digital home exercise program for back, hip, and knee pain.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain scores (measured on a scale out of 10), self-reported functional scores (strength, mobility, and coordination scores), and adherence rates.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Initial self-reported pain scores decreased significantly from an average of 5.19 out of 10 (SD 1.96) to an average of 3.35 out of 10 (SD 2.38) after 12 weeks. Post hoc analyses indicated a particularly emphasized pain score reduction over the early use phases. Participants with back pain showed significant improvements in strength and mobility scores, whereas participants with hip or knee pain demonstrated significant improvements in their coordination scores. A high yet expected attrition rate was observed across all pain areas and durations.

studies.intervention_and_control_group: High use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), and sub-LU use profiles of a digital home exercise program.
studies.additional_info_about_trial_arms: Patients were categorized into four use profiles based on their adherence to a digital home exercise program: high use (HU), intermediate use (IU), low use (LU), or sub-LU.
studies.collected_data: Self-reported pain intensities; achievement of clinically relevant pain improvement.
studies.key_outcomes_findings: Higher use profiles show a higher probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement of self-reported pain intensities. Additionally, the achievement of any higher use level is associated with a significant increase in the probability of achieving a clinically relevant improvement.
